So I have fixed all of these issues and may issue another pull request in a bit, that said testing with Travis-CI has uncovered a strange bug that appears to be latent.  I added the following test:

`    def test_esipy_expired_response(self):
        operation = self.app.op['get_incursions']

        with httmock.HTTMock(public_incursion_expired):
            warnings.simplefilter('ignore')
            incursions = self.client_no_auth.request(operation())
            self.assertEqual(incursions.status, 200)

            warnings.resetwarnings()
            warnings.simplefilter('error')

            with self.assertRaises(UserWarning):
                incursions = self.client_no_auth.request(operation())
`
and this passes testing, except on versions of python before 3.4 - the error is that the second assert fails with "AssertionError: UserWarning not raised". After a lot of testing simply commenting out the first call of self.client_no_auth.... has this test case passing.  At this point I'm pretty sure it's not my additions. 

If I edit an existing test case like so:

`    def test_client_warning_header(self):
        with httmock.HTTMock(public_incursion_warning):
            incursion_operation = self.app.op['get_incursions']
            self.client_no_auth.request(incursion_operation())
            warnings.simplefilter('error')

            with self.assertRaises(UserWarning):
                self.client_no_auth.request(incursion_operation())
`
This will fail with an identical error but only under pythons <= 3.3 . If I remove the first call to client_no_auth it will again pass.

虽然不知道为什么原作者不在`Windows`下开启多进程模式，但是自己改写了一个粗略版。
思想就是暴力多进程，而且有可能严重消耗内存，适合数据量大同时主机内存空间较大使用
```Python
#-*-coding:utf-8-*- 
import jieba
from multiprocessing import Pool,cpu_count

def cut(sentence):
	if sentence!=None:
		sentence = jieba.lcut(sentence,cut_all=False)
		return [i for i in sentence]
	else :
		return None

if __name__ == '__main__':
        path = raw_input("Enter the path: ")
        f = open(path,'r')
        # 读取数据
	data = f.readlines()
        f.close()
        # 创建进程池
	pool = Pool(cpu_count())
	data = pool.map(cut, data)
	pool.close()
	pool.join()
```
这里要求data为可迭代对象，返回值为多维列表

> These changes revert everything done to make gmpy2 a python package. This breaks tools and use cases which require a python package, binary wheels with dynamic linking most important one.
> 
> Furthermore, Installing library headers as data_files is problematic. It means they will be installed under site-packages/src/.. and will not be found under the package's install directory. So cython C level imports (#include <..>/gmpy2.h) will not be able to find the headers under the gmpy2 package directory.
> 
> On a lesser note, it is considered bad practice to install package files like headers as data files. The gmpy2_install_data stuff does not look correct for binary wheel installations either and I'm not sure setting install dir as if the package was pure python is correct as well.
>

The Curio's public API is actually much closer to IOCP than it is to Unix (e.g. in IOCP, `sock.sendall` is a primitive), so I suspect that the way to minimize impedence mismatch is to create an abstraction layer that looks like IOCP, implement curio's public API on top fo that, and then implement the abstraction layer using IOCP on Windows and selectors on Unix-likes.

Ok, thanks for the clarification. I'll be considering the `args_type = 'MODULE'` to be out-of-scope for testing, since it's an incomplete feature.

I've figured out the issue. The problem was that in some cases the end user was receiving a `_SinonStubCondition` object, and that this object was not initializing `base.py` attributes like `pure_count` and `args_type`.

Here's the solution I'm proposing:
1. Remove `pure_count`, since it seems that it hasn't been necessary since we added the `SpyCall` class
2. Add `@property` methods to `_SinonStubCondition` that indirect to the `_copy`.

Best Regards,

--Jonathan

Hi @nick13jaremek, thanks for the excellent PR. 

The tests use [betamax](https://github.com/sigmavirus24/betamax) to record requests against the Zendesk API and replay them during testing. The betamax configuration lives here - https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/tests/test_api/__init__.py. Essentially, it is configured to record a new interaction the first time it occurs, and afterwards to replay the recorded interaction instead of talking to Zendesk's servers.

You will need an empty Zendesk account to record the initial interaction, and if you place a file containing the test account's credentials at `~/zenpy-test-credentials.json` they will be used for any new interactions. Just commit the files that betamax creates alongside your new tests and everything should work fine.

In theory you should only need to add a new class in https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/tests/test_api/test_create_update_delete.py to test the CRUD operations against SupportAddresses.  

The command to execute the tests is `nosetests -v --stop`, and it must be executed in the root of the project in order to work correctly.

I hope all that makes sense, let me know if you have any questions!

There is no way to manage transmission end.  If you kill the program you kill the program.  It is not reasonable to assume that the bridge destructor would have some kind of data acknowledgement since that might hang forever waiting for acknowledgement from the other side that it got the data.  It should be obvious that the bridge doesn't do this since the bridge destructor doesn't hang.

It should also be noted that even if the bridge gives all it's data to the OS and all the data looks "sent" that doesn't mean the other host eventually gets the data.  If your application terminates the OS is perfectly allowed to drop those outgoing data buffers without sending them, which is often what it will do if you terminate your process before those OS networking buffers are done sending.  There are also a lot of other ways the data might not eventually arrive at the other side.  So you have to wait for some kind of acknowledgement back from the other end if you want to be sure it got the data.

[Sorry for the delay in responding.  I'm in the middle of changing jobs and moving across the continent, so my availability going to be somewhat sporadic.]

Jason, encrypting the entire file is the simplest secure approach, but as you mentioned, "it is the goal of the Crypto keyring to protect the passwords and not other data in the file."  I don't think it's such a great idea to maintain an unencrypted list of servers that a user has access to (OpenSSH hashes its ~/.ssh/known_hosts entries to avoid this) but I'm willing to treat that as a given for now.

The problem with passwords is that they're almost always cryptographically weak (low entropy and frequently reused), so it's really important for any program that handles passwords to leak as little information as possible about them (e.g. their length and their equality with other passwords).   Such a program should also make retrieving password somewhat computationally expensive (e.g. by using something like PBKDF2 with many iterations).

I had actually suggested encrypting all of the *passwords* together, not the entire file.  For example, you could make a JSON array of the passwords, encrypt that, and then put array indexes into the cleartext.

Regarding your points 2, 3, and 5 above, I agree with them in principle, but I think that getting the file format right should trump the other considerations.  For example, if the BasicFileKeyring API does not allow for the file format that we want, then the API---not the file format---should be changed.  Also, avoiding loading all the passwords into memory is nice if possible, but since the master key is already in memory during decryption, it would be wiser to secure the case where an attacker *doesn't* have access to the master key, than to prioritize the case where the attacker has access to the master key but is somehow unable to use it to decrypt the rest of the file.  We should also be careful to avoid making the system more complex just to implement some "nice-to-have" security features that are more likely to contain bugs than they are to prevent a successful attack.

If I were going to implement this for python-keyring, I would probably do something like this:

1. Given the user's master password MP, a random salt MS, and an iteration count i=2000
2. Use PBKDF2-SHA256(MS, MP, i) to generate a 256-bit master key MK
3. Use the master key to derive other keys:
  a. EK = SHA256("EK:" + MK)  # password encryption key
  b. V = SHA256( "V:" + MK)  # verification value
4. Store MS, i, and V as plaintext in the global portion of the file.  This is enough to recover the master key from the master password, and to check whether the correct master password has been entered.
5. For each password:
  a. Generate a random IVp for encrypting the password
  b. Encode the password as <length> + ":" + password, then pad it to a multiple of 64 *bytes*
  c. Encrypt the padded password using EK as the key and IV as the initialization vector (use CBC mode or CTR mode, but definitely not ECB mode), Cp
  d. Base64-encode the IVp and Cp, then store IVp ":" Cp in the individual password field.

Does that make sense?

I think the layering division of…

1. Selection (Decide what source value to use, among env vars, files, etc.)
2. Transformation (Turn the selected source value into whatever representation is convenient to the program: an int, a fancy object, a list of strings, etc. While we're at it, validate (which might be a separate layer but probably not).)
3. Presentation (Make the transformed value conveniently available where the program needs it.)

…might be an aid to thinking here. In my mind, those are the concerns that any config lib might address. Explicitly decoupling them in a library would make it usable in more situations.

It also might be freeing, in temrs of later places to hang bits of API, to represent as many things as first-class objects as possible. For instance, if we had a first-class representation of a config option, say, `app_config.debug_option`, we would have the option of hanging something like `debug_option.validate(…)` off that. This really comes into play with "compound options", like "everything you need to address a RabbitMQ server". You can define its validation rules once, then make a copy of that option group for each Rabbit server you need to configure.

Alternatively for my opinion parsing in normal mode should not be so difficult. It's better to do it this way because if you want to change config (xcommand) input must be normal mode and XML won't be reconized. A single parsing function can support all possible command output. To parse in normal mode, the idea is to use nested disctionary (see http://stackoverflow.com/a/16333441 as a example).

- first loop : read line one by one (lines = re.split("\n", output))
- parse each line with regex pattern "^(.*:) (.*)$", matches=re.search(r"^(.*:) (.*)$", line);
- matches.group(0) will be path, and matches.group(1) will be value 
- split path using space, remove first item ("*s" in your example)
- second loop : create empty dict, for each key, if key not in dict exist create it, use loop on key to create nested dict, when all key as been reached, put value in this location

nhentai advertises these files as *.png in every way possible:
- Image URLs end in *.png: https://i.nhentai.net/galleries/1164085/4.png
- The `Content-Type` header says it's a PNG file: `Content-Type: image/png`
- nhentai's gallery metadata says it's a PNG file: `…{"t":"p","w":1280,"h":1854}…` (meaning **t**ype: **p**ng)
- The files inside the download torrent also have *.png as filename extension

It is obviously a `.jpg file, but gallery-dl is only doing what it is being told by nhentai.

A possible solution would be to look at the first few bytes when downloading and see if the file header matches the suggested filename extension, but implementing this properly might take a bit.

Hi

First, some comments:

- Avoid `while True` loops; they will peg the CPU at 100% until the user selects an option. This package has a lot of functions that do that for you, and more efficiently.
- To get a selection from the user, try `keyboard.read_hotkey()`. It returns the next key (or combination) the user presses, as a string.
- If you wrap your code in triple backticks, Github will format with proper indentation.
- Note this library is for *global* key events. This is useful if you want to react to key presses even if your application is not in focus. If you don't need this, try the standard library functions, such as `input` or `getch`.

If you really need the full power of this library, and not just get console input from the user, I see two possible solutions.

One is to setup each option as a hotkey, which are executed asynchronously, and implement the timeout with a simple `sleep`, or `keyboard.call_later`. The code would look something like this:

```python
import keyboard
import time

accepted = False
def accept():
    global accepted
    accepted = True
    keyboard.remove_all_hotkeys()

def option1():
    accept()
    print('option1')
    # More code.

def option2():
    accept()
    print('option2')
    # More code.

keyboard.add_hotkey('1', option1)
keyboard.add_hotkey('2', option2)

time.sleep(10)

if not accepted:
    keyboard.remove_all_hotkeys()
    print('timed out')
```

The other option would be to use queues and/or threads, similar to to how `get_hotkey` is implemented (
https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/e63e54a956c7dcbc11ded11098e53460d0b1297f/keyboard/__init__.py#L936 ).

Unfortunately neither of which are very simple and easy for a beginner.

>  I'm still researching on how to get the current state of those keys.

This can be done using [GetKeyState](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms646301(v=vs.85).aspx) with VK_CAPITAL, VK_NUMLOCK and VK_SCROLL. The least significant bit is set if they are toggled on.

```python
>>> GetKeyState(VK_CAPITAL) & 1 #caps lock currently off
0
>>> GetKeyState(VK_CAPITAL) & 1 #caps lock currently on
1```

In 0.7.5 I switched to the pysam implementation of tabix to avoid calling out to the shell. But it looks like `pysam.ctabix.TabixIterator` does not include newlines when yielding lines and therefore is not the drop-in replacement for a file-like object that I thought it was.

2d7d176 fixes this and adds a regression test.

An alternative workaround is to call `.saveas()` on the value returned from `tabix_intervals()`, so:

```python
import pybedtools

bed = pybedtools.BedTool("test.bed.gz")
snp = pybedtools.BedTool("8\t129186110\t129186111\trs72722756", from_string=True)

intersection = (
    bed
    .tabix_intervals('{}:{}-{}'.format("8",129186110,129186111))
    .saveas()
    .intersect(snp, wa=True, wb=True)
)

for feature in intersection:
  print feature
```

I realized that most people don't want `goto_definitions` to work as it does. They are rather irritated by it. What you want is basically a `goto_assignments` function in its current state and a `goto_assignments` function that does it over and over again until it's not possible anymore. The latter would follow imports, obviously. And maybe also statements (if it's not a tuple or a builtin object).

The command could therefore look like:

The ghost messages you can get as follows:

1. launch «ip mon link» in some terminal
2. create a dummy interface --> ip monitor will show RTM_NEWLINK
3. delete the interface --> ip monitor normally will show RTM_DELLINK

But sometimes it should RTM_DELLINK and immediately RTM_NEWLINK:

```
# ip link add test type dummy
(monitor): 
2496: test: <BROADCAST,NOARP> mtu 1500 qdisc noop state DOWN 
    link/ether 86:2f:70:06:86:a9 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff

# ip link del test
(monitor):
Deleted 2496: test: <BROADCAST,NOARP> mtu 1500 qdisc noop state DOWN 
    link/ether 86:2f:70:06:86:a9 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
2496: test: <BROADCAST,NOARP> mtu 1500 qdisc noop state DOWN 
    link/ether 86:2f:70:06:86:a9 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
```

Thus any program that relies on these broadcast messages will be sure that the interface immediately gets recreated just after removal. It is a kernel 2.6 «feature», that can be observed on RHEL6.5.

On newer kernels starting from 3.0 there is no such «feature». And since IPDB work with broadcast messages, I have to handle it.

**If you're on Windows**
1. First, you need to check that you have ffmpeg/avconv installed somewhere, and that you know where it is.
  - The path you need to be concerned about is the one that directly contains `ffmpeg.exe` or `avconv.exe`
  - For example, in this situation, I need `C:\ffmpeg\bin`: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8049998/38163261-404863d2-352c-11e8-83f4-fe5dcaf25f35.png)
2. You need to get to your environment variables listing to add ffmpeg or avconv to your PATH.
  - You can press WinKey + Pause/Break to open your system information
  - You can then open the environment variables listing by clicking 'Advanced system settings' and pressing the 'Environment variables' button
  - Click the 'PATH' listing and select 'Edit'
    - If your version shows both user and system-related lists, select the one in the system list.
  - Add the path you found before to this list.
    - On newer versions of Windows, you can just press 'New' and give the path from before. Move this entry to the top of the list.
    - On older versions, PATH is a semicolon-separated list. Add the path from before to the start, separating it from everything else with a semicolon (so that it looks like `C:\ffmpeg\bin;C:\Windows\...`, for example.)
  - Press OK as many times as necessary to exit out of the settings completely.
3. You need to close all open command prompts (and optionally reboot) to apply the new environment variables for the next command prompt session.
  - Once you've closed everything, open up a new command prompt (`cmd`).
  - Type `ffmpeg` or `avconv`. If you get a bunch of help spam, you've updated your PATH correctly.
4. Try running your bot from a new command prompt window.
  - If you usually start your bot from an IDE, you may need to close and reopen the IDE to refresh its environment variables (in a similar fashion to the command prompts)
  - If you're using avconv, remember that you need to tell the library you're doing so: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8049998/38163411-b367079a-352e-11e8-9064-5cd6074185bf.png)
5. ffmpeg or avconv should now be working and usable by your bot.

**If you're on Linux**
1. You need to check you have ffmpeg/avconv installed, and that you know where it is.
  - The path you need to be concerned about is the one that directly contains the `avconv` or `ffmpeg` executable (or a symlink to it).
  - For example, in this situation, my path of concern is `/home/myuser/ffmpeg/bin`: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8049998/38163470-af7dfee4-352f-11e8-812b-f1e12e7a9fd1.png)
  - If this path is a standard location such as `/bin`, `/usr/bin`, `/usr/local/bin` then you may not need to edit PATH at all, and can skip directly to 3.
2. You need to edit one of your shell setup files to add the ffmpeg/avconv directory to PATH.
  - Unlike Windows, PATH isn't a single variable that simply gets spawned into every shell session. It is instead initialized by the various programs that create your shell environment.
  - Bash has two files, `~/.bashrc` and `~/.bash_profile` that it, and some other shells, use to set up the environment. `~/.bash_profile` is the preferred option, as it is used in more cases.
  - You can edit these files using `nano` or etc to include `export PATH=/home/myuser/ffmpeg/bin:$PATH` (substitute my example path for your own) to add your ffmpeg/avconv path to PATH whenever your shell loads.
  - You can then use `source ~/.bash_profile` to immediately apply the effects to the current shell.
  - If you use a shell not compatible with Bash, you may need to look up the alternatives for these files and commands.
3. Type in `ffmpeg` or `avconv` into your shell. If it comes up with a lot of help spam, you've added ffmpeg to your PATH correctly.
4. Try running your bot from your shell.
  - If you're on a graphical install of Linux, and are using an IDE, you may need to set up additional things in the IDE itself to apply environment variables if restarting doesn't automatically apply them.
  - If you're using avconv, remember that you need to tell the library you're doing so: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8049998/38163411-b367079a-352e-11e8-9064-5cd6074185bf.png)
5. ffmpeg or avconv should now be working and usable by your bot.

This error has to do with the backing store used for offset commits. How have you configured your server offset storage?

Debugging invalid certificate chains is always a bit tricky. You would have to look at the error message returned by SSLyze (which is one OpenSSL returns): "unable to get local issuer certificate". AFAIK this one means one of the two following things:
* The CA/anchor cert is not in the trust store
* An intermediate cert is missing so the full chain can't be built
Somewhat unrelated but certificate validation will be slightly better in the next version as I'll update to OpenSSL 1.0.2 which performs certificate validation in a way that's closer to what browsers do.

As far as I know there is no mechanism to pass an IP address to use to a CONNECT proxy; the proxy will always do DNS resolution itself, based on URL's hostname. It's not a limitation in SSLyze but in how proxies work.
One workaround in your example would be to pass an IP address as the `hostname`; this will force the proxy to connect using this IP address.

Hi @harisb! Yes you can ([demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/856bzzeL/943/))! First you set up the [`headerTemplate` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#headertemplate) to add an icon... * It will automatically add an `` when you set it to `{content}{icon}`. The `` will get the class name set in the [`cssIcon` setting](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssicon). ```js headerTemplate: '{content} {icon}' ``` * Or you can add custom HTML, and again, the class name needs to match the [`cssIcon` setting](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssicon). ```js headerTemplate: '{content} ' ``` Then set the `cssIcon`, [`cssIconNone`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssiconnone), [`cssIconAsc`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssiconasc) and [`cssIconDesc`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssicondesc) options ```js cssIcon : 'fa', cssIconAsc : 'fa-sort-amount-asc', cssIconDesc : 'fa-sort-amount-desc', cssIconNone : 'fa-sort' ``` If you're using a custom theme and the filter widget, make sure to include this definition: ```css .tablesorter .filtered { display: none; } ``` \* Note \* The `filtered` class is set by the [`filter_filteredRow` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#widget-filter-filteredrow) .

That is definitely a concern - and I had the same one before! [Github automatically redirects](https://github.com/blog/1508-repository-redirects-are-here), so no links are broken. It sounds like the move from GoogleCode to Github was challenging because of the completely different architectures, but thankfully moving around from within Github doesn't work like that. The history, links via url and also pushing to github, are also maintained with the repository - to lose it you would have to create a new repo, clone your repo, `rm -rf` the `.git` folder, and then push to the new one. I don't think this should ever be done. One of the beautiful things about Github, and the sole reason to use it, is version control and having a complete record and changes, and that would be destroyed with such a move. For your local repos, you can simply edit the `.git/config` to call the organization repo `upstream` and your fork still `origin`. For example, my singularity config looks like this:

```
[core]
        repositoryformatversion = 0
        filemode = true
        bare = false
        logallrefupdates = true
[remote "upstream"]
        url = git@github.com:singularityware/singularity.git
        fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
[remote "origin"]
        url = git@github.com:vsoch/singularity.git
        fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
[branch "master"]
        remote = origin
        merge = refs/heads/master
```
and we typically follow the [Github flow](https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/), so that master of the upstream (the organization branch) is always the "production ready" version, and PRs are sent to a development branch, which is merged into master when we want to do a new release (and this would coincide with a python-based software to be when you are ready to upload a new version to pip. Importantly, I'll also point out that no one has write access to the  organizational branch except for the one other developer and myself, and we **still** maintain the practice that I am never the one to merge, etc, he is lead. It's also very easy to protect branches, and give different users in the organization different levels of permission. For pydicom, I would suggest that you be the only one to have write access, and take on the role of lead. 

You are correct that there could be no benefit at all, and you could hate it and ultimately move the repo back, and it would be like you never moved it.  But I think there are more things to gain than lose in creating a stronger sense of community, inviting all the people that you already work with to be members of the association, and then having more eyes look over PRs, etc.

So here is what is happening: 

When using a service account config all requests are authenticated as an admin by default, including streaming. When making standard requests like get() we refresh the service account auth token before each request which ensures that we don't use stale tokens (tokens only last 1 hour). 

However, when we make a streaming request it is a single request that lasts as long as we are running the stream, and if we stream for over an hour the auth token becomes stale and Firebase sends us an event telling us about it instead of the sending us the data we are looking for. This happens even if our security rules for read and right are set to "true".

To resolve this we will have to figure out how to apply fresh auth tokens to our stream session without closing the stream itself.

First thing, thanks for the quick reply! 

I came into needing this in while writing an Artifactory wrapper for python 2.7.
I had a list of Aritfact objects such as
``artifacts = [ Artifact("my-product-1.3.5"), Artifact("my-product-1.3.6"), Artifact("my-product-2")] ``
But their ordering was by the Last Modified, not by the product name, so naturally I wanted to use natsort to get the latest artifact deployed:

``latest_artifact = max(artifacts)``

Which is cleaner than: 

``latest_artifact = max(artifacts, key=natsort_keygen(SOME_ALGORITHM_HERE))``

because it encapsulates the sorting algorithm within the class itself. 


However, with how natsort currently works the __cmp__, __gt__ or __ge__ methods will have to be implemented in the following manner:
```
def __gt__(self, other):
    return natsorted([self, other]).index(self) == 1
```

Which is *really* ugly. (__ge__ will be implemented in the same way and __cmp__ will be really really ugly.)

This seems like the right thing to me.  `current_instrument` is used to keep track of the most recent program change seen on each channel in a given track.  This is useful for determining which `Instrument` to associate with each completed note.  `last_note_on`, on the other hand, should be used for determining notes which appeared on a given track/channel/pitch prior to seeing a note-off.  So, we still need both, but it makes sense (after 0ca39fd1b1558468a402f1cf8fa126ce4ed7e76a) to use `event.channel` as the key rather than `current_instrument[event.channel]`.  I think this change was basically missing from 0ca39fd1b1558468a402f1cf8fa126ce4ed7e76a.

This example replays weeks from a dataset made up of days (the same concept applies when going from minutes to days when it comes to the `Close` order)

The logic issues a BUY order on Wed (or Thu/Fri if Wed happens to be a non-trading day) and sells on Mondays.

The BUY execution takes place on "Friday" (a couple of times on Thursday, when Fri was not a trading day) with the closing price, because Friday is the day the weekly bar is closed. But during the week, the strategy had the chance to issue an order.

The dataset is in the backtrader sources.

Code (adds a sentinel to avoid concurrent BUY orders)

```python
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,)
#                        unicode_literals)

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds
import backtrader.indicators as btind


class St(bt.Strategy):
    def __init__(self):
        self.order = None

    def notify_order(self, order):
        if order.status in [order.Completed]:
            dtstr = bt.num2date(order.executed.dt).strftime('%a %Y-%m-%d')
            if order.isbuy():
                print('BUY EXECUTED, on:', dtstr)
                self.order = None
            else:  # Sell
                print('SELL EXECUTED on:', dtstr)

    def next(self):
        dtstr = self.data.datetime.date(0).strftime('%a %Y-%m-%d')
        weekday = self.data.datetime.date(0).weekday()  # Mon 0 - Sun 6

        if self.position and weekday >= 0:  # sell at beginning of next week
            print('SELL CREATED, on:', dtstr)
            self.close(exectype=bt.Order.Close)

        elif self.order is None and weekday >= 2:  # order on Wed/Thu/Fri
            print('BUY CREATED, on:', dtstr)
            self.order = self.buy(exectype=bt.Order.Close)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    cerebro = bt.Cerebro(runonce=False, preload=False)  # both False for replay

    data = btfeeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname='2005-2006-day-001.txt')
    cerebro.replaydata(data, timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Weeks)
    cerebro.addstrategy(St)
    cerebro.run()
```

> "The broker offers me after the market is opened a fix price for a few seconds.. " 

May the problem be that you test with daily bars but want execution based on intraday events? It seems so.

> "with this price I can calculate the number of shares"

You calculate the number of shares and not the real broker. The same is done in the platform which tries to remain as realistic as possible.

If you have "a fixed price for a few seconds" this is an intraday bar at the opening of the session. During this bar you can calculate the number of shares you can acquire. Or a sizer like in #72 can do it for you, but based on an actual price.

In remaining realistic backtrader cannot look forward for preloaded prices to make a calculation for you, because "preloading" is an artifact intended for performance and not to cheat looking into future prices.

Version 1.5.0.x has added live data feed. Should live data feeds also support giving you a non existent price (the next opening price) to support your strategy?

This seems like an intraday vs daily backtesting issue.

`order_target_percent` is what many banks offer: *tell me how much you want to invest and based on the "ACTUAL" price I calculate the number shares for you". But it needs an "actual" price and not a "future" price.

I was hoping for a bumpversion hook for autotools. When you update the version number, you need to run autoreconf, which propagates the version number to all the various configure files so that configuring creates the proper version number. I'd rather not go behind autoreconf's back and try to do its job for it. 

This mean's I'd like bumpversion to:

1. Update the files I want with the proper version number (in my case, configure.ac).
2. Call a script of my choice. (In my case, autoreconf -fiv, which propagates the version number to the appropriate places and creates a git commit.)
3. Create a commit and tag for a new version, etc.

My two bits' worth. I could theoretically write a script to do this: call bumpversion --no-commit --no-tag, run autoreconf -fiv, then bumpversion. But a hook system would make that easier, just set an option for the autoreconf -fiv hook and let bumpversion handle things.

> The contract that you probably care about as a consumer of IFoo is that do_async returns an awaitable, but it's perfectly possible to write a non-async function that returns an awaitable.

I agree, however this is a pretty unorthodox way of writting async function... well orthodoxy about async function have changed a lot since Python 3.4, but now async/await have landed and are supported by all major frameworks (asyncio, twisted, tornado, curio, trio etc.)

So considering your example, I would expect it to be implemented like:

```python
class ValidFoo(interface.implements(IFoo)):

    async def do_async(self):
        return await MyAwaitable(5)
```

This way we respect the async interface requirement, even if we use a custom awaitable.

On a more theoretical point of view, async/await has been designed to easily recognize asynchronous blocks and synchronization points (otherwise generator with yield would have done the job fine ^^). I would say defining interface share a similar goal as another way of making Python code more explicit (as opposed to just have something that runs).

I don't know the right way to handle this. There are two ways to install from git:

 1. You can install in normal mode, then it clones to a temporary directory and runs `setup.py install` in that dir:

        pip install git+https://github.com/GrahamDumpleton/wrapt.git

 2. Or you can install in "editable" mode which does the checkout and just creates an egg symlink. In that case, the package needs to be named as expected inside the checkout.

        pip install -e git+https://github.com/GrahamDumpleton/wrapt.git#egg=wrapt-dev

The latter is what I'm trying to get working. It appears that one option is to specify [a different root package](https://docs.python.org/2/distutils/setupscript.html#listing-whole-packages) like `package_dir = {'': 'src'}` which would just require moving `src` to `src/wrapt`.

That's probably the best option to preserve the behavior you want of avoiding importing from the local source directory, while still allowing `pip install -e` to work as expected. If you're interested then I'll update the PR.

FWIW I don't know how much `pip install -e` matters overall. For me it's just a stop gap while waiting for a release with #54 merged.

I feel a little uncomfortable with a sneaky setup.py as it can lead to unexpected behavior for users. For example, one might expect the `method=pocketsphinx` option to be available for audio files, but it wouldn't be if you didn't have `swig` installed prior to `pip install textract`.

Having said that, `swig` is fundamentally different than, for example, `antiword` or `tesseract` for .doc and audio files, respectively. textract will install without `antiword` or `tesseract` being installed and textract throws an error if those executables are not also installed.

I could imagine incorporating `extras_require` into the setup.py for `pip install textract[swig]` to enable the pocketsphinx audio processing option. I'm not wild about the `[swig]` nomenclature but at least its explicit about that dependency. The main difference this option needs to exist is because pocketsphinx needs to link to swig libraries at installation time. What do you think?

It would be nice if we could instead have `pip install textract[no-swig]` so that the default is to install textract with pocketsphinx support, but it appears that this isn't encouraged by python convention so I'd rather not rock the boat on this unless I'm misunderstanding things.

In addition to updating the setup.py, we should probably also update the [installation instructions](https://github.com/deanmalmgren/textract/blob/master/docs/installation.rst). If you take care of all of that, I'll figure out how to re-work the `requirements/` directory to make it easy to support these sorts of things in the future.

Yes the idea was to math the `plot` method in Pandas. That is where the actual `kind` keyword came from instead of the common `type` form in Plotly figures. 

It was also meant to be for very quick charting, where arguments are interchangeable across chart types (which is not entirely the case in `figures` directly) so any user could just change the chart `kind` on the fly. 

However, I agree the code in `iplot` has grown a bit too much. And with some many `kwargs` it has become difficult to understand. 

I like the idea of having a method for each figure type. It may be challenging in some instances, as it will seem to overlap with native `DataFrame` methods. For example `DataFrame.hist` which is Panda's own histogram method. 

The `iplot` method would still remain - and every standalone method would just call `iplot` with the relevant arguments. Not sure if this wold help massively for code readability, but it will certainly help for documentation purposes. 

This would only apply to regular charts, and not to `QuantFigure` charts.

TIP: One 'hack' that I just came up with was to write a small Python script to make use of the docker-py API to extract the history from an image and locate the latest one with a 'tag' associated with it, and return the hash / id for that layer. I'm not sure how robust this logic is, but in my ad-hoc tests this results in the layer ID for the layer of the base image used by the current docker file, which in turn can be passed to the '-f' parameter of docker-squash.

Here's a little snippet of what I've done:

```
# get_hash.py

# ... put code here for parsing image / tag name from command line

from docker import Client
def get_hash(image_name):
    cli = Client()
    hist = cli.history(image_name)
    for i in hist:
        # NOTE: Ignore the first layer in case it has been tagged as part of the Dockerfile build process 
        if i is not hist[0]:
            if i['Tags']:
                return i
    return None
```

And here's an example of how one might use this (requires a *nix shell):

```docker-squash -f `./get_hash.py old_tag_name` -t my_new_tag old_tag_name```

I thought about this problem a bit and I found that a tree data structure would be a fitting solution for it. Trees are recursive and there are well-developed algorithms for traversing. With the idea of swarms inside of swarms, this screams "recursive" to me. So my idea is to create a tree with the root being the highest layer and the leaves being just particles. Every layer in between is filled with swarmparticles. In this case, every "node layer" would then be a swarm. I have drawn a fancy picture to visualize this (note that the children are *not* limited to 2)
![skizze](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/39431903/42124098-2be0ac7e-7c5d-11e8-9fb0-b5c626133b62.png)
So in every layer, we can initialize a swarm with the existing `swarm` class. We then just need to implement a tree data structure where we can store the swarmparticles for every swarm.
To traverse the tree we can use a stack (or [depth-first search](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tree_traversal#Data_structures_for_tree_traversal)) approach. In this way, we can compute every swarmparticle's best position first and use this information in the layer above as the `pbest` of the higher-level swarmparticle.
I think this might work without creating a whole bunch of new classes. The `swarm` class can be left as it is, we just have to insert the position and velocity of the swarmparticle, which can be calculated using the mean of the positions and velocities I guess. The only additions we'd have to make are the tree structure and maybe a new `Optimizer` class.

Hey, main author of Trio here. I wrote some more notes here: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/170#issuecomment-410907876

Trio does check that the sockets you pass in are `socket.socket` objects – this is to avoid things like allowing a `ssl.socket`, which is a subclass of `socket.socket` but with different and incompatible behavior (from the point of view of an async library like trio). But, this isn't actually a problem at all, because mocket *also* patches `socket.socket` to refer to its special object, so that test passes :-). AFAICT there are two issues:

* Because of a strange quirk, trio tries calling the real `socket.socket` methods on the object that get passed in, and these get confused because *they* require a real-honest-to-goodness `socket.socket` object. (Hence the "descriptior 'send'" bit the original bug report.) There's more discussion of this in the thread linked above.

* Since Trio is an async library, it needs to be able to pass sockets to low-level OS primitives like `epoll_ctl` and `select`. It looks like mocket is not set up to support this, so even if we fixed the descriptor issue it still wouldn't work? I see what looks like an attempt to support this in `MocketSocket.fileno()` calling `os.pipe`, but I don't see how it can work (maybe it assumes that no-one ever calls `fileno` more than once? also it obviously only supports Unix, since on Windows pipe and sockets are totally incompatible – `socket.socketpair` might work better).

However, trio does have explicit support for socket mocking at a slightly higher level: if you have a class that implements the *trio* socket API (which is basically the regular socket API except the blocking functions are marked as async), then you can tell trio to use that instead of regular sockets. I wonder if there would be any way to implement a class like this, while reusing mocket's existing code for handling http requests etc.?

:+1: I have an additional issue that would be solved by having this take over as the official django-storages PyPI package. Consider the scenario where package 'ds-dep' depends on django-storages as part setup.py and you want to use django-storages-redux. If you have django-storages-redux listed before ds-dep then pip will install django-storages-redux, install the dependencies for ds-dep, and then install ds-dep. However, since django-storages-redux does not meet the requirements for django-storages, which ds-dep depends on, it will be overwritten by the PyPI django-storages package. This can lead to subtle and confusing bugs.

There are two ways to get around this currently, neither of which is desirable. First you could list django-storages-redux AFTER ds-dep which will overwrite the python site-packages/storages folder. This results in the correct backends getting used. The second method is to use the '-e' method and ensure the egg is named django-storages. This is more desirable since subsequent dependencies of django-storages will see that it has been satisfied. The down side is you are now pulling from github instead of PyPI.

I had a try at your solution and I agree the dict-based `parent_lookup_kwargs` is how things should be. 

One this that has caught me off-guard is the need to specify the lookup for the object itself:

This is better explained with an example. From your example earlier in this thread, you had mentioned:

``` python
   parent_lookup_kwargs = {
        'parent_pk': 'parent__pk',
        'parent2_pk': 'parent__parent_pk',
        'parent3_pk': 'parent__parent__parent_pk'
    }
```

However, the following is required for the reverse URL lookup to work:

``` python
   parent_lookup_kwargs = {
        'pk': 'pk',
        'parent_pk': 'parent__pk',
        'parent2_pk': 'parent__parent_pk',
        'parent3_pk': 'parent__parent__parent_pk'
    }
```

Notice the `'pk': 'pk'` entry. This is needed because all URLs for object details have a parameter for the object's primary key.

The issue is coming from django-tagging using django.contrib.contenttypes.
Contenttypes was not using migrations before Django 1.8 and tagging neither.

So when the migrate occurs, Django do a syncdb on models without migrations, but because contenttypes has now migrations, the creation of the tables for tagging could not work anymore.

Since tagging use migrations and know his dependency to contenttypes, the issue does not occurs anymore when creating a fresh database.

@nuevoleonkx 
Consider lifecycles:
- app: one instance from app start till the end
- sio: almost as long as the application (it gets attached to the application somehow)
- request: often created and then destroyed, with 10 requests per second there are 10 requests per second created.

For this reason (request has very short lifecycle), I guess, passing request to sio is the wrong direction. I would say, the request handler shall get access to sio and call its relevant method instead.

There can be also multiple requests attempting to use single sio instance.

It is unacceptable in the sense that it is not fit for interactive use. For a node with over 2300 descendants it takes ~6 seconds to load the fully recursed serialized data and another almost 2s to render the tree. The issue is made even worse by the fact that this is a navigation menu, so the user will start at the top level, pretty much guaranteeing they will hit an item with many descendants and hitting these response times. Serializing a node one level down with 260 descendants takes less than 800ms, that is not good, but acceptable.

What I ended up doing is that I've added a helper serializer that only serializes a single item and use it in a method to serialize a list of items I get from MP_Node.get_children and call it in the actual serializer using SerializerMethodField:

```
class ChildrenSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Item
        fields = ('id', ...)

class ItemSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    children = serializers.SerializerMethodField()
    class Meta:
        model = Item
        fields = ('id', ... , 'children')
    def get_children(self, obj):
        data = [ChildrenSerializer(x).data for x in obj.get_children()]
        return data or None
```

This only goes to the next level down and takes less than 150ms for the node with 2300 descendants as it only has 6 children. But this method feels hacky and it would be cleaner and more readable to just be able to write something like:
`children = serializers.ListField(child=RecursiveField(depth=1))`

@piiswrong 
Sorry, I think the score of invalid anchors is assigned to -1. The CPU implementation uses unstable-sort (`std::sort`), but GPU implementation uses stable-sort(`thrust::stable_sort_by_key`). It leads to inconsistency in invalid anchors.

The cpu/gpu consistency couldn't be guaranteed when the output has invalid anchors whose score is -1.

https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/contrib/multi_proposal.cc#L89
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/contrib/multi_proposal.cc#L141
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/contrib/multi_proposal.cc#L159

I followed Proposal Operator and Multi Proposal (GPU) to implement Multi Proposal (CPU).
The output of Proposal Operator may have invalid anchors, but invalid anchors is needless. (reference: 
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/example/rcnn/rcnn/symbol/proposal.py#L133)

There are three ways to solve the cpu/gpu consistency test in Proposal and MultiProposal:
1. Ignoring the comparison between invalid anchors whose score is -1
2. Using stable-sort for the CPU implementation
3. Removing invalid anchors from the output.

Right, but it isn't *your* code so having detailed explanations of jQuery Mobile's use of an undocumented internal feature isn't going to help. As the end user programmer, the information I *think* you want is, "What version of jQuery Mobile fixes this?" At the moment there is none. In another ticket you mentioned that you wanted the name of the file so you might infer where the problem is. I assume the next step for you would be to go and either report the bug or find the version that has already fixed it.

> Unless jQueryMobile is using some undocumented feature, in which case it would be entirely jQueryMobile's fault and not Migrate.... no wait, if that was the case, using Migrate wouldn't prevent the error.

The reason Migrate fixes the problem with Mobile is that it shims `$.event.props` to allow older code to work. jQuery Mobile doesn't *add to* props but does "borrow" the props list for its own use. I don't know of any other code that does that. We might be able to put in a getter for `$.event.props.concat` to give a message for this particular situation.

Yup, zmq Contexts and Sockets cannot be used across the fork boundary. You must create new Contexts when creating new processes.

If you really want to create a socket, send a message, and block until it's done you can use a fresh Context for each:

```python
ctx = zmq.Context()
s = ctx.socket(...)
s.send(...)
s.close()
ctx.term() # this is where it blocks waiting for LINGER
```

Flag 1024 means that it failed to find the closest reference sequence. My guess is that for each cluster there is no nucmer match bewteen the contigs and the reference sequences. This should be in the log file around the text "Looking for closest match from sequences within cluster". You could do extra checks by using the `--noclean` option and analysing the assemblies yourself - they are called `clusters/*/assembly.fa`.

Assimp's FBX import code actually does have a section which is specifically inverting the PostRotation matrix before use.

I'm looking into this and it's problematic because either (a) the geometric transforms get inserted in between the main node and a new node containing each mesh, or (b) the geometric transforms are inverted before attaching child nodes.

With (a) the original node no longer contains any mesh, which is problematic as the node name in FBX seems to be the mesh name. If that node doesn't contain any meshes it would likely cause all sorts of problems in user applications.

With (b) there's a whole bag of worms if the geometric transform is animated.

I think i'll just assume it will never be animated and use (b). From the example FBX file provided by @wmatyjewicz above, the geometric transform is indeed marked as *not* animatable. As geometric transforms are a feature pretty-much specific to 3DS Max, probably this can be vaguely relied on.

I just did a quick test. @rozhok boto3 will automatically read those credentials without specifically configuring fetching them, even while running in a container in an ECS cluster.

In my particular case, I failed to set the `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` environment var as I was operating not in `us-east-1`. Once I did, all it took for me to use instance meta data creds was:

```
client = boto3.client("s3")
client.list_objects(Bucket="com.mybucket.things")
```

Just to reiterate again, to use the instance meta data (by assigning an IAM role to my instance at launch), I just needed to initialize boto3 as documented and it automatically read the creds in the meta data. In my case, I was operating outside of "us-east-1" so I also needed to be sure I set `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` in some appropriate way:

- `env AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-west-2 python my_command.py`

- `os.environ["AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"] = <read from a configuration file>`

Below is the script which will display all the Hosts in a given cluster if cluster is not provided displays list of all clusters in the vCenter. 
`

    #!/usr/bin/env python

    """List Of Hosts in a Cluster."""
    from pyVmomi import vim
    from pyVim.connect import SmartConnect, Disconnect
    import atexit
    import argparse
    import getpass
    import ssl
    import requests

    def get_args():
        """Get arguments from CLI."""
        parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
            description='Arguments for talking to vCenter')

        parser.add_argument('-s', '--host',
                            required=True,
                            action='store',
                            help='vSpehre service to connect to')

        parser.add_argument('-o', '--port',
                            type=int,
                            default=443,
                            action='store',
                            help='Port to connect on')

        parser.add_argument('-u', '--user',
                            required=True,
                            action='store',
                            help='Username to use')

        parser.add_argument('-p', '--password',
                            required=False,
                            action='store',
                            help='Password to use')

        parser.add_argument('--cluster-name',
                            required=False,
                            action='store',
                            default=None,
                            help='Name of the cluster you wish to list the hosts')

        args = parser.parse_args()

        if not args.password:
            args.password = getpass.getpass(
                prompt='Enter password')

        return args


    def get_obj(content, vimtype, name=None):
        return [item for item in content.viewManager.CreateContainerView(
            content.rootFolder, [vimtype], recursive=True).view]

  
    def main():
        args = get_args()
        # Disabling urllib3 ssl warnings
        requests.packages.urllib3.disable_warnings()

        # Disabling SSL certificate verification
        context = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1)
        context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE

        # connect this thing
        si = SmartConnect(
            host=args.host,
            user=args.user,
            pwd=args.password,
            port=args.port,
            sslContext=context)

        # disconnect this thing
        atexit.register(Disconnect, si)

        content = si.RetrieveContent()
        for cluster_obj in get_obj(
                content,
                vim.ComputeResource,
                args.cluster_name):
            if args.cluster_name:
                if cluster_obj.name == args.cluster_name:
                    for host in cluster_obj.host:
                        print host.name
            else:
                print cluster_obj.name


    # start this thing
    if __name__ == "__main__":
        main()
`

But also I have to admit that the old architecture was not very good and many things which where private should have been public. Let's think what can be done with your problem and can we make some of the methods public. Ed example had a need for the `ElementFinder` class, because he did want to overwrite some methods to better support AngularJS extension. 

@GLMeece could you find/grep your code and see which private methods you need from the S2L? Then we would have a better picture which private methods you actually need and can better decide how to solve the problem. As a quick thought, perhaps we could do something similar than what we did do with the `ElementFinder`. One can access the class from the library instance, it is here: https://github.com/robotframework/SeleniumLibrary/blob/master/src/SeleniumLibrary/__init__.py#L262 
If we provide access in similar way, would it satisfy your needs?

But please look into your code and tell if there is a need for other private methods.

```
class Component:
    motor = wpilib.Talon


class MyRobot:
    left_component = Component
    right_component = Component

        left_component_motor = wpilib.Talon
        right_component_motor = wpilib.Talon

```

This is the code, which is a bit verbose. Indeed there is more than one consumer, in fact two of them, simulateneously consuming from different topics and one producer as well.

// pk_owner.py

    import logging
    import time
    from datetime import datetime

    from resources import make_kafka_client, make_zk_client

    logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

    kafka_client = make_kafka_client()
    zk_client = make_zk_client()

    class SessionState(object):
        def __init__(self):
            self.memo_table = None
            self.lock = zk_client.Lock("/pk_lock", "act")
            self.topic = kafka_client.topics["pks"]
            self.consumer = self.topic.get_simple_consumer(
                consumer_group="default",
                auto_commit_enable=False
            )
            # demands setting topic.log.retention.hours to forever ("-1" ?)
            self.consumer.start()
            time.sleep(0.1)         # waiting as consumer starts off
            self.initial_consumer_offset = 0
            for _, partition in self.consumer.fetch_offsets():
                # assumes there's only one partition
                if not partition.offset == -1:
                    self.initial_consumer_offset = partition.offset
            if not self.initial_consumer_offset == 0:
                logger.debug("starting with non-zero offset of %i", self.initial_consumer_offset)
            self.producer = kafka_client.topics["pks"].get_producer()


    session_state = SessionState()

    def get_pk(pk):
        def load():
            offset = [offsets.offset[0] for offsets in session_state.topic.latest_available_offsets().values()][0]
            logger.debug("preparing to fetch %i records", offset)
            for i, message in enumerate(session_state.consumer):
                session_state.memo_table[message.value] = message.offset - session_state.initial_consumer_offset
                if (i + 1) % 10000 == 0:
                    logger.debug("%i records already fetched", message.offset)
                if message.offset + 1 >= offset:
                    break

        if session_state.memo_table is None:
            session_state.memo_table = {}
            logger.debug("requesting lock | 1")
            with session_state.lock:
                logger.debug("entering lock | 1")
                load()
                logger.debug("leaving lock | 1")

        if pk in session_state.memo_table:
            return session_state.memo_table[pk]

        logger.debug("requesting lock | 2")
        with session_state.lock:
            logger.debug("entering lock | 2")
            # making sure pk is still unknown to all other workers
            load()

            if pk in session_state.memo_table:
                return session_state.memo_table[pk]
            
            # adding pk to the feed
            session_state.producer.produce([pk])

            time.sleep(0.1)     # waiting enough for our change to propagate
            
            # getting the id for pk
            load()

            if pk in session_state.memo_table:
                return session_state.memo_table[pk]
            
            logger.debug("leaving lock | 2")

        raise RuntimeError("leaving without a pk")

@stefanv @jni @JDWarner 
The flow has changed significantly now. I added the optimizations Stefan suggested. Now the function does try to remove as many seams as possible in one go. As far as I have observed, low energy seams come from a localized region, and they are more likely to intersect. Given that, multiple simultaneous removal results in about 20% speedup.

The main speed gain with the changes, is by taking an energy map, instead of a function. Although I thought otherwise earlier, pre computing energies does produce similar results to that of recalculating at every iteration. Also, if anyone does want to recompute energies, he/she can use our code and remove one seam at a time.

As each of the copies above is replacing each other, I don't expect this PR improves memory usage much if at all.  It should improve performance a bit.

I rescaled the cameraman image up to size 4096x4096 and ran `skimage.measure.label` on it.  Prior to this PR it took 773 ms and afterwards it takes 626 ms (as reported by %timeit).  

I think the main reason for the high memory usage reported in that mailing list thread is that the labels are stored in int64 dtype.  This means that the labels are 8 times the memory usage of a uint8 input.  I don't think there is much way around that without rewriting the routines to allow the label array to mach the integer dtype of the input (perhaps via fused types?).

I don't intend to pursue that in this PR, but it is probably worth still merging it for the minor performance boost.

Interestingly, on Python 3, `str(b'abc')` is `"b'abc'"` (i.e., the repr), but as soon as you add *any* errors parameter, you get a nice normal str back: `str(b'abc', errors="notfound")` is `'abc'`.

Our use of the `unicode`/`str` constructor never specifies an encoding, so we'd be using the system default, typically ASCII. That seems wrong. Can we just switch to UTF-8 or Latin-1? (For our existing test cases, latin-1 can properly decode all the strings---which it should be able to, it can decode any set of bytes; they just might not print super pretty. UTF-8 for the existing test cases invokes our `zope.error.printedreplace` handler.)

Also some of our tests for the `zope.error.printedreplace` error handler are incorrect. For example, 
```python
self.assertEqual(u'\\u0441', self.getPrintable(b'\u0441'))
```
produces the warning "Anomalous Unicode escape in byte string: '\u'", so it's not actually encoding anything. It doesn't even actually hit the error handler. In fact, the only test that does hit the error handler, `test_ErrorLog_nonascii`, doesn't seem to check to make sure that the results can be round-tripped. Is that even important? If these are just for display, why don't we use `surrogateescape` (A: That's Python 3 only) or`backslashreplace` (A: That's for encoding only) or `replace` (A: That *kind-of* loses information?) or a handler that produces the character name ?

The argument order (especially, when using multiple APIs with different ideas) is quite a headache.

To avoid it, I'd suggest to use `Point` instances (rather than tuples) whenever possible, and always construct them using named arguments. `vincenty(Point(latitude=y1, longitude=x1), Point(latitude=y2, longitude=x2))` may look overly verbose but is ultimately error-prone and doesn't require any backwards-incompatible API changes. BTW, converting to `Point`s is [essentially what geopy does](https://github.com/geopy/geopy/blob/9a44bc3/geopy/distance.py#L240) when you pass a tuple. `measure` is called from `Distance.__init__`.

I mean, how many projects out there would become broken if some future version of geopy would be released with the change you propose? Forget to pin the version in `requirements.txt` and you'll have a hard time debugging why your spatial searches had suddenly went haywire.

I'd suggest:

1. `Point` should accept abbreviated `lat` and `lon` kwargs. Possibly, allow `lng` spelling as well. Full words are nice but cumbersome, and I doubt anyone would be confused by abbreviations.
2. It would be good to introduce a pair of shortcut helper functions, `latlon(y, x)` and `lonlat(x, y)`. I believe they would be especially useful when some foreign API gives one a tuple: `lonlat(*coords)` looks explicit (almost to the extent of being self-explanatory) yet concise.
3. Documentation should be updated to strongly suggest avoiding raw tuples and using `Point`s whenever possible. Maybe even to extent of `DeprecationWarning` with some major release. All examples should follow this suggestion.

Just my personal opinion.

print(location.latitude, location.longitude)
print(location.raw)
print(location.address)
```

The mostly Airy MTF we've used for PPL, which has a sampling factor similar
to WFIRST H-band, has some numerical artifacts outside the bandlimit that
are O(10^-5).  For frequencies about 50% above the bandlimit, the errors
are O(10^-6) or smaller.  I'm not saying that's the requirement -
unfortunately, what IMCOM can handle is a matter of trial and error at this
point.  But a good guess would be a 10^-5 limit.

Let me know if you think we need to prioritize changing IMCOM to enforce
the bandlimit itself - I need to leave this issue be unless it becomes
pressing.  But even though this change will allow IMCOM to construct a
valid solution from non-ideal PSF input, the images themselves would still
contain the spurious high frequencies at some level.  Shape measurement
accuracy on the oversampled images would need to be verified.

Chaz

>>> res = tower_cli.get_resource('job_template')
>>> res.list(inventory=1)['count']
8
>>> res.list()['count']
9
```

In this usage, you can't put in the inventory name as an alternative. The reason for this is tricky. What you have in this case is the actual `list` method, not the click command. In order to get the full functionality that corresponds to CLI use, there would be some other additional overhead, and that's something that I would like to have.

Alternative, the query parameter can do most of anything you might ever want.

```python
>>> res = tower_cli.get_resource('job_template')
>>> res.list(query=[("inventory__name", "Farmington_animals")])['count']
8
>>> res.list()['count']
9
```

This does the same thing as the prior snippet, it just leverages the Tower query logic to reference by name. The fact that you would have to provide the query as a list of tuples.. that's more than a little non-obvious. But this reflects more of the internal mechanics of the command rather than a good user-facing interface.

You can combine with other parameters, such as `res.list(query=[("inventory__name", "Farmington_animals")], all_pages=True)['results']`, to return all inventories that use that inventory, even if those filled more than 1 page.

> switching to python 3 on the CASD is risky and costly and we won't be able to do it in the summer or the autumn.

I'm suspecting that several of OpenFisca reusers will have the same issue. So I think OpenFisca will have to be compatible with Python 2 ou 3 for at least a couple of months.

To this aim, I'd suggest the following methodology:
- Try to run OpenFisca with python3
- Run into an issue
- Fix it in a way that works **both** for Python 2 and 3.
    - If there is a way of doing things that works for both version, I'd suggest using it instead of a more fancy python-3 way.
    - If we end up using new features brought by python3, polyfills (such as enum34) can be used
    - In the other case, and `if`/`else` block is always possible. 
    - In all case, the interface of OpenFisca should be the same in python 2 and 3.
- Move to the next issue 🚅 

I'd suggest _not_ to wait to have made the whole code python-3 compatible to start worrying about retro-compatibility. This strategy could lead to higher costs, as part of the work may need to be undone.

Since 0.5, the input variables of a formula are no more declared as formula parameters but are called during formula execution.

To retrieve the input variables of a formula you now need formula introspection. I just implemented it in package [OpenFisca-Parsers](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-parsers).

This package contains a [module to retrieve the input variables of a column](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-parsers/blob/master/openfisca_parsers/input_variables_extractors.py) and a [sample script that uses it](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-parsers/blob/master/openfisca_parsers/scripts/extract_input_variables.py).

If you install this package and upgrade OpenFisca-Web-API, the /api/1/field API has also been updated to optionally show the input variables of a formula. For example, for variable "revdisp": http://localhost:2000/api/1/field?variable=revdisp&input_variables=1

> I checked the issue and the fill_value is not propagated (it takes the default 1e+20 for float arrays). So I think we should explicitly set the fill_value. There may be contexts where it can get handy to know the fill_value directly from the data. What do you think?

The issue I was referring to is that the fill_value is not propogated when masked arrays are operated on.  For example, see how the fill_value of the `output` variable varies below.

```
import numpy as np
a = np.ma.array([1,2,3], mask=[False, False, True], fill_value=999)
b = np.ma.array([1,2,3], mask=[False, False, True], fill_value=888)
c = np.ma.array([1,2,3])

output = a + b  # output.fill_value is 999
output = b + a  # output.fill_value is 888
output = a + c  # output.fill_value is 999
output = c + a  # output.fill_value is 999999 (sometimes...)
```

I agree that setting the `fill_value` of a masked array is prudent, but I would not rely on it being correctly set when retrieving the value.  Using `np.ma.filled` with an explicit `fill_value` parameter is a more reliable method for creating a ndarray with a particular value where the original data is masked.

**Installation**
Requirements : python and pip properly installed

$ pip --version

If you don’t have python and/or pip installed, this [Python installation guide](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/starting/installation/) can guide you through the process.

**Download memote**
The sources for memote can be downloaded from the Github repo.

You can either clone the public repository:

`$ git clone https://github.com/opencobra/memote.git`
Or download the tarball or zip archive:

`$ curl  -OL https://github.com/opencobra/memote/archive/master.zip`

**Install memote**
We highly recommend creating a Python `virtualenv` for installing memote to avoid some installation trouble.`virtualenv` is a tool to create isolated Python environments: itcreates a folder which contains all the necessary executables to use the packages that a Python project would need.

Install virtualenv via pip:
`$ pip install virtualenv`

Test your installation
`$ virtualenv --version`

Create a virtual environment for installing memote:
```
$ cd my_project_folder
$ virtualenv my_project
```

Activate the  virtual environment:
`$ source my_project/bin/activate`

Install memote in the virtual environment
`$pip install memote`

Note that you can directly run this last command if you do not want to install memote in a virtual environment.

**Using memote**
After installation, memote can be employed in two different ways: As a benchmarking tool for ad hoc model assessment and as an automated testing suite to aid with the reconstruction of metabolic models....

That sounds good. We can have an explicit `begin` which users can chose to call to get a transaction id and then they can interleave reads. If they do not call `begin` then the first `read` or `query` will create the transaction and if they try to interleave before it has returned they will get back an error.
So essentially users can interleave reads only after there is a transaction id which can be got by either calling `begin` or by the first `read`

`def _DecodeUrlSafe(urlsafe):
  """Decode a url-safe base64-encoded string.
  This returns the decoded string.
  """
  if not isinstance(urlsafe, basestring):
    raise TypeError('urlsafe must be a string; received %r' % urlsafe)
  if isinstance(urlsafe, unicode):
    urlsafe = urlsafe.encode('utf8')
  mod = len(urlsafe) % 4
  if mod:
    urlsafe += '=' * (4 - mod)
  # This is 3-4x faster than urlsafe_b64decode()
  return base64.b64decode(urlsafe.replace('-', '+').replace('_', '/'))`

yeah. to mirror `pip` et al, it'd be ideal if the pex could install all distributions into something resembling a shared `site-packages`-like dir within the pex. as you mention tho, this is likely to break several key assumptions within pex around things like runtime platform resolve checking, etc which depend on the pex containing all requisite "resolvables" rather than their "installations".

furthermore, I think you'd also need multi-universe permutations of this shared `site-packages`-like dir for all combinations of runtime interpreter version, platform/abi, etc - or you could tease out universal vs platform-specific dirs and then compose them via `sys.path` (but that might also break some packaging assumptions that expect shared composability). at any rate, keeping both the full contents of the `.whl` file itself *and* its "installed" representation with permutations is likely to bloat the size of produced pex files.

one possible solution here might be to simply treat any whl-bound deps as a variant of not-zip-safe and then construct, cache and use a shared `site-packages`-like dir at runtime in the `PEX_ROOT` path (e.g. `~/.pex/wheel_install/<fingerprint>/*`). assuming a wheel-specific impl, I think this should be pretty reliable and quick enough to do at runtime - particularly if cached.

For now, you will have to do this with an extension function or similar approach.

The general idea is:

1. Get the paragraph element (`w:p`) from the paragraph object

    ```python
    p = paragraph._element
    ```

2. Retrieve the run elements using XPath

    ```python
    rs = p.xpath('//w:r')
    ```

3. Construct your own Run objects from the `w:r` elements:

    ```python
    runs = [Run(r, None) for r in rs]
    ```

This is air code, so you may need to work out the finer points. But this is how I would approach the problem.

This is theoretically possible, but not with PyDrive. 
You could use a setup similar to [this](http://stackoverflow.com/a/16696317/3918512) to download the file in chunks. Then you can, in each iteration, upload each chunk to Google Drive using the REST API, chunked upload example [here](https://developers.google.com/drive/v3/web/manage-uploads#chunking) - you need to click 'Expand for more info'. So, it'd look something like this (pseudo-code a la Python):

```python
# Set up, and authenticate the connection to Google Drive.
download_url = '<your download URL>' 
total_size = 1000  # Would need to gain this information from the download url.

# Create file object on Google Drive with correct total size, file name etc. and gain a session URI (needed for the header sent in the for loop below)  
session_uri = '<your session uri>'

current_position = 0  # The byte position in the uploaded file, where the next block will be inserted
r = requests.get(download_url, stream=True)  # From the SO post for chunked download.

for chunk in r.iter_content(chunk_size=1024): 
    if chunk:
        chunk_length = len(chunk)
        # Make resumeable upload request at position `current_position` of length `chunk_length` to Google Drive, 
        #    as illustrated in the documentation. 
        # The header would contain the session URI, 
        # The line `Content-Range: bytes {current_position}-{current_position + chunk_length}/{total_size}`
        #   and: `Content-Length: {chunk_length}` -- see documentation for all required fields.

print("Upload completed.")  
```

The fact that neither PyDrive nor the google-api-client library for python support this functionality makes this a lot more difficult than it looks. If you figure this out, make sure to share it here :)

Side-note: You may still be able to use the google-api-client to authorize and gain a session URI, however, quickstart instructions [here](https://developers.google.com/drive/v3/web/quickstart/python).

> Is there any reason why the jpmml tfidfVectorizer throws an exception when norm is not None? 

This exception is thrown to inform you that normalization in general, and the "norm" attribute in particular, is not supported at the moment. If the converter simply ignored this fact, then the PMML representation of the Scikit-Learn pipeline wouldn't be exact/correct, and you'd be getting mismatching predictions later on.

I remember that I have discussed the background of this "not supported" decision someplace else before. In brief, Scikit-Learn calculates term frequencies all in one go, whereas (J)PMML calculates them one by one, as they are needed (kind of lazy evaluation approach). It would be terribly inefficient to perform normalization over the complete vocabulary - (J)PMML would need to invoke term frequency calculation both for "active" and "inactive" terms. In most cases, the majority of terms fall into the "inactive" category.

You should consider rearranging your Scikit-Learn pipeline to perform normalization after you've identified which terms are "active":
```Python
pipeline = Pipeline([
  ("generate terms", TfIdfVectorizer()), # Complete vocabulary
  ("select terms", SelectKBest(k = 500)) # Identify/select 500 "active" terms
  ("normalize terms", Normalizer()) # Perform normalization over a subset of terms
  ("classifier", RandomForestClassifier())
])
```

Would the explicit normalization using the `sklearn.preprocessing.Normalizer` transformation work for you?

Some estimator types contain "internal" normalization logic, so the explicit normalization step might be unnecessary/redundant.

> And also, when making this change to the python side, do I have to make any changes to the java side as well?

The Java side **must** reproduce the prediction logic of the Scikit-Learn/Python side.

In other words, if some Python class attribute is significant from the prediction logic perspective, then the converter must look it up, and generate appropriate PMML markup for it.

> Could having normalization the way I have added it, without any change to the java side, potentially cause any logistic problems?

When in doubt, compare Scikit-Learn predictions with (J)PMML predictions over a representative data set.

This is caused by the way in which `DidlMetaClass` is declared as a metaclass of `DidlObject`. I don't think we can really blame Sphinx, since what we are doing is rather hacky, to attempt to achieve Py2/3 compatibility.  

Replacing
```python
class DidlObject(DidlMetaClass(str('DidlMetaClass'), (object,), {})):
```
with
```
class DidlObject(object):
    __metaclass__ = DidlMetaClass
```
makes the doc build work on Python 3, but of course it then breaks on Python 2. It may be that using `six` or `python-future` fixes it. I haven't tried yet.
