@lyw07 I'm on Ubuntu 16.04 also, and I don't have `/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/OpenSSL/`. 

Check out the last part of the traceback:

```
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/OpenSSL/SSL.py", line 118, in <module>
    SSL_ST_INIT = _lib.SSL_ST_INIT
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'SSL_ST_INIT'
```

Reading through the code of `OpenSSL/SSL.py` and `OpenSSL/_util.py`, I find the definition of the `_lib` coming from `cryptography`, hence the problem is that our bundled in `cryptography` is conflicting with a system library.

```
from cryptography.hazmat.bindings.openssl.binding import Binding
binding = Binding()
ffi = binding.ffi
lib = binding.lib
```

The only remedy is to either:

1. Create our own bundled `OpenSSL` package as well, to override system's `python-openssl` package
1. Replace our bundled `cryptography` and replace it with a dependency on Ubuntu's `python3-cryptography`.

Btw. your problem won't exist with our `kolibri` Ubuntu .deb package, because it only runs in Python 3, so it won't get confused by your `python-openssl` package.. however that's a short-term solution since Python 3 is taking over in later Ubuntu releases.

So escaping only left-angles (and even right-angles, too) isn't enough for passing to `html-tidy`.

Here is the issue I just filed about it: https://github.com/htacg/tidy-html5/issues/542

If it's straightforward to add a custom filter to escape characters like this, I assume that would be enough.

After some testing on my end, I think that it might be unnecessarily complex to do this at install time, particularly because if someone builds from a .whl or binary things could get messy.

Instead my proposal would be to add the following functionality:
1) Allow the use of a flat config files that are simple editable text files.
2) Create a method in sascfg.py that the user can call one time (or many times) to create a default_config.ini flat text file
3) Allow the user to specify the config filename when they create a `SASsession()`

Many people do not follow the "put everything into a separate `virtualenv`" installation strategy. Here is a possible way of their thinking process:
 1. There already is the whole SciPy stack installed on my system, I do not want to wait for it to compile and occupy extra space. I'd rather add `--system-site-packages`. 
 2. So, I will not be using the `pip install -r requirements.txt` approach but I'd rather construct the `virtualenv` myself. 

At this point there is a series of `pip install somelib`, where `pip` fetches the latest versions of those libs and you really could not be bothered to compare those versions with the `requirements.txt`. Or, even if you are, it is common to think that if the version of the required lib is older than the available one, it is probably because the list of dependencies is a bit outdated and not because there actually is a problem with the latest version of that library.

> I was also considering: is it better to use "async def" etc syntax in here if we're going to use "python3-only" syntax at all? Or is it better to go the *other* way and only use txaio?

I think this hits the nail. Either one way pure (async def) or the other way (txaio). The `yield from` syntax was transitional only in Py I think.

So I think the story to pitch to _users_ (with no "legacy" code base or restrictions) should be: go Py 3.5+ and use the new syntax.

However, _in general_, as a _library_, the "we support every Py" story is a valid and strong one, and for Twisted users I would say this is important.

Now, for asyncio the situation is probably different. Boils down to: what is the asyncio user base sitting on 3.3/3.4 and on 2.7/trollius.

If we go for new syntax within AB/asyncio, we should officially deprecate AB/asyncio/Py<3.5 though. At least ask on the mailing list before. So this aspect maybe deserves some discussion / feedback ..

The problem, as I see it, with jumping through hoops to solve a conflict with system python is that any OS X update could cause a new or recurring issue. For example, OS X (MacOS!) 10.12 is coming next week--who knows what issues that will bring...

For the SDK itself, I don't see any need to push people towards using homebrew, or even providing any specific installation advice. If you're developing software with python you should be following best practices (homebrew, virtualenv, etc.) anyway.

But in the context of the CLI, I think most people are going to want to have it installed globally instead of needing to start a virtualenv every time they want to use it, so in that case, saying "hey, you should install python from homebrew, ignore at your own risk" is totally fine. If someone who is "non-technical" can get themselves to a shell and deal with installing and using the CLI, I think they can certainly handle install python via homebrew.

I have added an `autocommit` option that you can provide before connecting to your database. That should help with connecting to Hive. The feature is part of turbodbc 1.1.2 which will be released in the next minutes by the CI builds I have triggered. Usage:

```
from turbodbc import connect, make_options

options = make_options(autocommit=True)
connection = connect(dsn="My Hive data source", turbodbc_options=options)
```

See here for more extensive [documentation](http://turbodbc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/advanced_usage.html#autocommit)

Update: According to [MySQL bug #67702](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=67702), you can work around by setting the connection option `no_ssps = "1"`. This will disable server-side prepared statements in favor of emulating prepared statements on the client side. This sounds like a very bad idea performance-wise.

I'll try to get a minimal reproducing example for the MySQL guys to chew on.

I think this change is a bad idea. In response to the original issue, if I install git in my root and then activate another environment, then yes, I absolutely do think that git should no longer be in my `PATH`. I don't think the root environment should be special in any way.  Users who are confused by this, to quote @kalefranz, should learn to use `which` and `type`.

In particular, conda's attempt to seal up its environment interacts badly with the two stacked environments in `PATH`. Suppose I'm in my root environment, its `bin` is in my `$PATH` (as conda encourages), and,

```sh
$ python -c 'import affine'
ImportError: No module named 'affine'
$ conda create -n foo python affine
$ source activate foo
$ python -c 'import affine'  # Yay!
$ ipython -c 'import affine'
ImportError: No module named 'affine'  # Huh??
```

Same thing for `jupyter-notebook`, `py.test`, and a host of other things. The problem here is not that I am ignorant of how `PATH` works. The problem is that conda has rewritten the #! lines in the programs I'm running, explicitly to ignore my `PATH`! If the first line of `py.test` was `#!/usr/bin/env python`, things would be fine—I'd be testing with the `python` I explicitly indicated I wish to use (by activating its environment and thus putting it at the front of my `PATH`). But conda, in trying to prevent me from shooting myself in the foot, has made me shoot myself in the other foot.

As a side note, in the example above, even if I figure out what's going on it still may not work.

```sh
$ conda install ipython
$ ipython -c 'import affine'
ImportError: No module named 'affine'   # Whaaaaa!?
$ which ipython
~/conda/envs/foo/bin/ipython  # I'm so confused...
$ type ipython
ipython is hashed (~/conda/bin/ipython)
```

What fraction of users understand that `which` != `type` and they may need to `hash -r` before their conda install takes effect? Shadowing is complicated, shadowing + caching is truly evil.

It might be a cool feature for conda to support stacked environments, but I think a lot of thought would have to go into getting this right. In the meantime, I don't think it's right to give special treatment to the root environment in `PATH`.

I'd take @njalerikson's proposal one step further: I think it would be cleanest if conda wasn't self-hosting, _i.e._ if `conda` was a program that would live in `/usr/bin` or somewhere else, completely independent of any python executable or environment at all. That would remove the obstacle from any specialness of the root environment. I suspect this is a rather substantial change, however.

It's not needed. Conda build automatically changes the paths of the libraries using install_name_tool when they are built, so that they are relative (look at `otool -L` on one of the library files installed by conda). If DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH were required, then an environment would *have* to be activated to be useable (meaning you couldn't just call the absolute paths to an environment). This further complicates things if you want to combine run programs from different conda environments. Also, DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH changes everything, not just the conda packages it is intended to change, meaning that setting it could break something else. It's not as likely as DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH, but still possible. The current approach with install_name_tool on the other hand only changes those libraries that specifically link against the dependent libraries to use relative paths. 

For your h5py, you will either need to use that DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH, or build a conda package for it with conda build (there is a conda recipe for h5py in the conda-recipes repo that you should be able to modify).

Basically some packages in the defaults channel are in an inconsistent state: one or more files listed in the package metadata (``info/files``) are missing. Conda attempts to link them anyway and fails.  I guess the real issue here is that reporting linking problems at installation time is simply too late. One solution would be change ``conda-build`` to validate the contents of each package as part of the build process. Or ``conda`` itself could try to validate the package it is about to install - but before making any changes to site-packages. Ideally, we could have both checks in place.

It seems like permitting both `X.Y` and `X.Y.0` files to exist breaks some assumptions and so normalizing the package name to PEP440 would at least prevent the same version from existing twice without different build numbers (e.g. you'd have to either use `--force` or increment the build number in order to upload). There's also a great many ways that PEP440 can normalize things, so it is probably very easy to accidentally duplicate packages where the versions are "identical".

I had another look at this and I believe it can be fixed by adding a synchronisation lock on the `handle_proxy_407` function. This would resolve the issue of multiple threads attempting simultaneous access to the relevant shared objects (`session.proxies` & user input).

However this then raises the issue of what if the user makes a typo. Currently, because `get_proxy_username_and_pass` is memoized and requests doesn't appear to distinguish between no credentials and bad credentials, conda will just retry the same username/password until your account gets locked out.

To get around this you have to check the credentials are valid before letting the other threads run again and add a method to signal `get_proxy_username_and_pass` not to use cached credentials.

Here is a working example: [fetch.diff](https://gist.github.com/patrick984/31261b15e0d912583e2f#file-fetch-diff)

I added an immediate check of the credentials within `handle_proxy_407` and 2 state variables: 
 - **attempts:** and integer which is incremented on authentication failure and passed as a second argument to `get_proxy_username_and_pass` to invalidate the cached values.
 - **authenticated:** a boolean flag to prevent the other threads needlessly re-checking the credentials.

Not sure if this is the most elegant solution but it does appear to work.

You can use the regular `Session` interface on its own, but the problem with doing so is that CockroachDB's transaction model is (partially) based on optimistic concurrency control, so your transaction may be aborted and need to restart (this is possible in any database due to deadlocks, but it's somewhat more likely in CockroachDB). The `run_transaction()` wrapper takes a callback so it can run the transaction multiple times if necessary. (it also uses our [client-side retry](https://www.cockroachlabs.com/docs/transactions.html#client-side-intervention) protocol to make these restarts a little faster than they would be with a naive restart of the transaction). 

By "no side effects other than writes to the database", we mean that you should not do anything externally-visible like sending an email or push notification, because the transaction function may be run more than once. Putting your entire request handler in a transaction would not be ideal (depending on how your web framework works) because you don't want to render your response until after the transaction is committed. 

I'm open to suggestions about how to integrate this more naturally with SQLAlchemy - I've never used the `Session` layer in a real project, just the lower-level query builder. FWIW, I think `run_transaction` will work with other backend drivers as well, so you can use it whether you're backed by cockroachdb or not.

This is RPM hell, as it is colloquially known.  Your options are to:

1. Install the RPM manually, instead of from the curator repo.  You would also need the other dependent RPMs in this case (click, urllib3, elasticsearch, etc.).

2. Install via `pip`, as per the [instructions](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/installation.html) in the documentation.

> Now I am able to only install curator python library but not the curator cli interface. Any hint on how to solve this?

Curator 4 does not separate the API and CLI.  They are the same.  If you can install Curator 4, you have the CLI.  It is just very different from Curator 3 as it uses YAML configuration files instead of a bajillion command-line flags.   Start [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/configuration.html) for more information.

They are very similar and I originally did conversions without it before eventually putting it back in. A few reasons:

1) the old style CommandGroup had this, so it makes it easier to convert modules that were already using this.
2) I tried this without at first and it was very annoying. You either had to have two command groups (one for SDK and one for custom) when logically they were just one, or you had to specify `command_type=foo_custom` for every custom command.
Rather than us writing custom logic for this, we should contribute to Knack or request them to support this. I don't think this is a feature that should only be added to our CLI.

As you can see here, the Storage 2016-01-01 API Version has changed from fixed "key1"/"key2" key names to a list of key objects where "key1"/"key2" are just regular name:
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/mt163589.aspx

This open the possibility in the future to decide to choose a customized key name (I don't say it's in any roadmap, just that this is now possible).

This means that even if the example shows the list in the "key1"/"key2" order, this is not a requirement and might changed in the RestAPI answer. In addition, the order might be changed by the implementation and might be inconsistent between C#, Java, Python, etc.
Thanks @lmazuel, I have my code updated.

From my perspective, it is because WSGI's abstraction is useful but not good with long-running requests (long poll/websockets), which is why it's basically just WSGI-with-async these days, albeit with all the unicode edge cases tidied up and more than just a request event.

Much like when WSGI was first introduced, there's no real reason for any single server to support it - as you said, you already have a good server and application abstraction - but it's that it allows frameworks and servers to be swapped out rather than being tied to each other, which is why I'm pushing it mostly from the framework side of things.

The problem is that `run_app` is blocking function.
You can use `app.make_handler()` though (see https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/2.3/aiohttp/web.py#L432) but proper initialization/finalizing code is not trivial.

In aiohttp 3.0 `run_app` is reimplemented on top of `AppRunner`: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web.py#L39
Runner is a container for running application, sites like `TCPSite` is responsive for handling specific TCP host/port .

The ideal from a security point of view would be to put it in an iframe always; otherwise the code to detect Javascript becomes a security target (evil me wants to include Javascript while tricking the detector into giving it the all-clear).

When a user views an HTML file inside Jupyter, we put it in a sandbox like this:

```html
<iframe id="iframe" sandbox="allow-scripts" src="{{file_url}}"></iframe>
```

That allows Javascript to run inside it, but treats it as being on a separate domain to the notebook server, so it can't do notebook stuff. (`{{file_url}}` is a template variable)

Both excellent proposals but I don't have good solutions yet.

On the zeros-vjp proposal: I agree that it was nice the way the zeros-vjps used to cauterize the graph Hydra (pinch the tips of the graph tomato vine?) instead of sprouting explicit zeros. The challenge is that it breaks the new tracer/autodiff abstraction barrier and I don't know the best way for tracer to expose it as a feature. We should definitely fix, but in the short term I'm not too worried because it only affects a small number of functions, like np.where. That might be not be the case for other numerical libraries though.

Having primitives defined separately for different tracing applications is also something we should do. There, I'm mostly worried about the overhead of checking each trace against the trace types supported by a given primitive. I should try implementing it and see how it looks. One thing to think about is what the interface should look like. It might be annoying to have to go through all the numpy primitives and tag them as primitive wrt to your new tracing application. But that's probably the cleanest way to do it.

_is_primitive is definitely cruft. I was playing around with putting a check in defvjp to confirm that the function is actually a primitive. Nothing deeper than that, I'm afraid.

Ah, that's a linker issue, nothing wrong with install. Just make sure /usr/local/lib (or wherever you installed libpostal) is on LD_LIBRARY_PATH. Add ```export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/lib``` to your bashrc and open a new ssh terminal to make it persistent across sessions, probably a good idea anyway since many C libraries install there.

If I understand it correctly, the issue is that, let's say you have hook A running and B queued and the user changes a config value.  So now your hook queue is A, B, C (where C is the config-changed).  A has a snapshot of the state from when it started, so won't see the new values, but when B runs, it may see the new config values even though C (config-changed) has not run.

Somehow I had it in my head that the snapshot was taken when the hooks were queued, but if they're taken when the hook is started I could see that being a problem.  It would be nice if there were a better way to handle this than saying "always use `@hook()` instead of `@hook('config-changed')`" (or `@hook('*')`) but I'm struggling to find a better solution.

At any rate, it should be easy enough to make the hook decorator behave that way.

See https://github.com/NSLS-II/databroker/pull/168 for a fix. I think we should still make Headers serializable, but that requires making Broker, FileStore, MDS, and all the "portable" variants of these serializable, so it will take some coordinated effort.

while not es.stop():

I think the only way you can obtain a certificate for the app engine is with the manual plugin in this way:

./letsencrypt-auto -t -a manual -d yourdomain.com auth

It will use the "file" challenge so it will show you a text string to put in a file. 
Before press enter you have to create this file and upload it in your app engine and you have to be sure that app engine knows this is a static file in text/plain mime type.

An easy way could be to add this to your app.yaml file:

- url: /\.well-known/acme-challenge/(.+)
  static_files: wordpress/.well-known/acme-challenge/\1
  mime_type: text/plain
  upload: wordpress/.well-known/acme-challenge/.*
  application_readable: true

Then you can press enter a letsencrypt should be able to check the challenge.
When finish you should have your certificate in your pc: in linux is in /etc/letsencrpyt/live/ folder. I am not sure where in MacOsX.

Before to be able to upload the script on the google cloud console you also need to convert the private key in RSA. I can do that with this command:

openssl rsa -in privkey.pem -out privkeyRSA.pem

Now you are ready to upload cert and privatekey and enable https in your app engine.

Yes, `setuptools_scm` is a pypa project for simplifying version number management, and it is installed if necessary. I'm not terribly worried about a small build-time dependency like this. The vast majority of people install from the universal wheel or through a platform's package manager (e.g. conda, debian, etc). I've heard no complaints about it and it makes dev versions in particular very convenient to differentiate between, which is useful for people who might be testing against master and opting in to unreleased features.

The major upside is that the version is specified exactly one place - in the SCM metadata - and everywhere else it appears is derived from that.

The major downside from my perspective is that the release version number does not actually appear in the git *data* in any way, so a snapshot of the library's contents from a given period in time, independent of the git repository, will not actually allow you to build the library as it appeared at that time. I think this is a minor problem, though I suppose with the right automation procedures it could be remedied in some way without doing it manually.

@umrashrf @Allactaga As I'm documenting this code, I realize that there's actually a simple workaround for this bug. Simply specify a `default` datetime. Currently, if `default` is `None`, it uses midnight at the current date and replaces whatever elements are specified. In the intermediate term, or when using old versions of dateutil, you can solve the problem by specifying the first of the month:

    from datetime import datetime
    from dateutil import parser
    
    datedflt = datetime.now().replace(day=1, hour=0, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
    date_out = parser.parse("Feb 2011", default=datedflt)

If I'm interpreting the error report correctly, It looks to me like the JSON you are passing is not valid. 

If this line:
```
-> 3295         if 'behavior' in _dict:
```
is throwing the exception
`TypeError: argument of type 'NoneType' is not iterable`

then it seems that `_dict` is `None`.

`_dict` was passed in as `_dict.get('next_step')`.  Now `_dict.get('next_step')` would evaluate to `None` if 'next_step' was not present in _dict, but we know that is not the case from the check on line 2564.  So it must be the case that 'next_step' _is present_ in _dict with an explicit value of None.

But 'next_step' must be an object with certain properties, such as 'behavior'.  So 'None' is not a valid value for 'next_step'.  Thus the input is invalid and an exception is the proper behavior in this case.

I'm going to claim that tqdm doesn't work on 2.6 because it's trying to write a unicode to stderr.

On my macbook, this is what happens in Python 2.7:

```
(p27)stone:~ brianb$ echo $LANG

(p27)stone:~ brianb$ python
Python 2.7.12 (default, Aug  8 2016, 10:53:11)

NameError: name 'sys' is not defined

And this is what happens on Python 2.6:

```

Python 2.6.9 (unknown, Mar  7 2016, 10:48:33)

Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import sys

'UTF-8'

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\u8175' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)
```

The tqdm code in the close method is clearly trying to write a unicode object.  This code remains in the master branch of `tqdm/tqdm`:

```
        def fp_write(s):
            fp.write(_unicode(s))
            fp_flush()
```

I think the essential information is:
- how fast is the transfer going
- how much longer will it take

and then "how much was already done" and a progress bar is a bonus.

We could do it like AWS does it, but personally, if I had a choice between what `tqdm` can provide for us (almost by default), I'd prefer our design. If we have a good idea on how to do this, then they should change their tool so that it looks more like ours does ;)

After reading this discussion, I set out to try solve this myself. I implemented a POC function

```python
def merge_schema_errors(errors):
    if errors and errors['_schema']:
        schema_errors = errors['_schema']
        del errors['_schema']

        for f in schema_errors:
            print(f)
            for field, field_errs in f.items():
                if field not in errors:
                    errors[field] = []

                errors[field].extend(field_errs)

    return errors

```

Which did what I wanted it to, provided I called `ValidationError` like this:

```
raise ValidationError({'fieldName': ['Error message here']})
```

This has a terrible interface, but my use case required it..

But, whilst finishing off writing my validation errors, I noticed the docstring for `ValidationError` specifies that you can actually provide a tuple or list of field of where to apply the message specified as the first argument of `ValidationError`

So without any extra code, I got this working by using 

```python
ValidationError('This is my validation error', field_names=['myField', 'myField2'])
```

I hope this clears things up for anyone looking here in the future.

I agree with you (@trickmeyer and @rueberger) that an OO interface would be nice. Let's brainstorm on what that could look like. Like you suggested, I think the basis should be an experiment base class that each individual experiment inherit from. Let's stick with `ExperimentModel` for now.

The user provided subclass would take the role of the current experiment object instance, by collecting functions, configurations, and some other settings. The main method (or commands in general) would be methods, and configuration entries could be accessible through `self`.

But departing from your suggesting I would have instantiations of that class take the role of the current run objects, by holding the (modified) configuration and settings, being executable, capturing stdout, and firing events for the observers.

That means config updates should be evaluated during instantiation of the class. That would suggest putting the configuration code into the `__init__` method. `ExperimentModel` could either provide a `self.config` or change the way  `__dict__` behaves, such that config updates block assignments. 
Putting the config code into the class definition (with or without metaclass support) seems suboptimal, because that code is executed when creating the subclass, not when creating the instance

So to a first approximation, it might look like this:

```python
from sacred.oo import ExperimentModel, main

class MyExperiment(ExperimentModel):
    def __init__(self, command=None, config_updates=None, named_configs=None, options=None):
        super().__init__(command, config_updates, named_configs, options)
        self.single = 1
        self.double = self.one * 2

    @main  # serves as a marker for the entry point
    def mymain(self):
        print("double of {} is {}".format(self.single, self.double))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    run = MyExperiment(config_updates={'single': 5})
    run()  # calls run.mymain() with stdout capturing and event firing
```

One potential problem with this is that the namespace of this object is used for many different things:  the methods of the experiment, the methods of the run, the configuration, and the custom methods. But other than that I quite like it. Integrating this would take quite some effort, but is certainly doable. What do you guys think? Is this what you are looking for?

While it's totally adequate to license full functional applications under the GPL, it seems that liberal licensed libraries benefit it the end due to a broader user base. It's more likely that issues and patches are contributed back even if the lib is used in non FOSS applications, than an entire application gets open sourced because of a single library license. In such a case it's more likely that decision makers move over to another library or write it's own.

So I think that LGPL (or even more liberal) is in general the better choice for libraries than GPL, and LGPL prescribes that changes/improvements must be published anyway.

Your view on this topic may vary though ;)

Yes, I have fixed the problem. 
The background is that Z3 uses a simplex engine that works over rationals. It has some support for bounds propagation for non-linear monomials on top of this. In the process some variables declared as integers have rational values. In the end, the solver is unable to determine sat/unsat. Z3 creates a candidate interpretation, nevertheless and this step did not take into account that the current rational values to integer variables was incorrect.

  o (str.substr String Int Int String)

    [[str.substr]](w, m, n) is the (unique) word w2 such that
    for some words w1 and w3
    - w = w1w2w3     - |w1| = m
    - |w2| = min(n, |w| - m)     if 0 <= m < |w| and 0 < n

    [[str.substr]](w, m, n) is the empty word if m < 0, m >= |w|, or n <= 0

    Note that the second part of the definition makes [[str.substr]]     a total function.

> This should not cause any change in behavior because the outputs of the previous true-division were always cast to integers.

There is a theoretical change in behavior: Casting a division result to `int` rounds towards zero, while floor division (`//`) rounds towards minus infinity:
```
>>> -13. / 4
-3.25
>>> int(-13. / 4)
-3
>>> int(-13. // 4)
-4
```
But the layer would break for negative downsampling factors anyway, so we can ignore this.

    l_rec_1 = lasagne.layers.ReshapeLayer(l_rec_1, (num_batch, seq_len, -1))
    l_rec_1 = LSTMLayer(l_rec_1, num_units=num_units_2)
    l_rec_1 = LSTMLayer(l_rec_1, num_units=num_units_3)

    # equivalent cell implementation
    cell_inp = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))
    cell = DenseLayer(cell_inp, num_units_1)
    cell = LSTMCell(cell, num_units_2)['output']
    cell = LSTMCell(cell, num_units_3)['output']
    l_rec_2 = lasagne.layers.RecurrentContainerLayer({cell_inp: l_inp}, cell)

The idea is that each cell can accept multiple inputs and outputs, and similarly for the container layer. Multiple inputs is already implemented by specifying which cell's input binds to which layer's input, which provides the sequence.

Comparison of matplotlib figures is certainly a feature that would be incredibly useful because it's such a common use case. I'm not sure of the best approach, though.

At the risk of slightly going off-topic I'm just going to do some braindumping here of various things that I have been thinking about in the past. I'd be curious to hear other people's opinions.

First of all, it would be nice to have at least the option of bitmap comparison in `nbval` (potentially using something like [perceptualdiff](http://pdiff.sourceforge.net/) with a given threshold).

If a matplotlib figure is stored in a suitable format internally (e.g. SVG) this might allow for more powerful comparison because it may be possible to report the difference in a more conceptual way, e.g. "the y-axis shifted by 3 pixels but everything else stayed the same". Also, I have written (and seen) tests before that compare `matplotlib.Figure` objects directly instead of exported images (using the internal structure of the figure rather than any output, i.e. how it is composed of axes, labels, etc.). Obviously this isn't possible in a notebook because the `matplotlib` object is gone, but I wonder if longer-term something like this could be a better approach for robust comparison of plots. All of these approaches (apart from perhaps `perceptualdiff`) are definitely outside the scope of `nbval`, though, and should potentially be discussed with the `matplotlib` developers instead.

A related idea is what the (highly recommended) [Holoviews](http://holoviews.org/) project are doing. One of their key ideas is that the visualisation is conceptually separate from the underlying data. In particular, the data itself is always stored inside the class that performs the visualisation and is therefore still accessible. For testing in `nbval` this could allow us to (i) compare the underlying data, and in addition (ii) check that the _type_ of visualisation used is still the same. This would lead to a successful comparison even if something minor changed in the output. Btw, the Holoviews developers use Jupyter notebooks for their tests and I know that in the past they had some trouble with robust comparisons and making the workflow smooth. It might be useful to drop into their Gitter channel and discuss things with them, they are a very nice and responsive bunch.

Finally, it would be fantastic to integrate `nbval` with something like [ApprovalTests](http://approvaltests.com/) (see [here](https://github.com/approvals/ApprovalTests.Python) for the Python version). The workflow I have in mind is that `approvaltests` calls `nbval` to compare a notebook and if a comparison fails (e.g. because an output image changed) then it would fire up something like [nbdime](https://github.com/jupyter/nbdime) to allow for a quick check of whether the change should be rejected as a failure or should be approved as the new "master" version to compare against in future tests. I'm not actually sure whether this would require much change in `nbval` at all - and it may just be a matter of hooking it up with `approvaltests` in the right way. Also, this approach could be nice for easy visual comparison of changes in `pandas.DataFrame`s and other complicated structures.

Anyway, lots of random thoughts. Bottom line is: I think that focusing on the _underlying data_ for comparison instead of any output (e.g. bitmaps) generated from the data is probably the most robust route in the long run.

So I just looked at the `read_table` code and I'm not sure the in its current state we could do a simple try/except solution. This is because the code is already doing try/except. Here are the relevant lines (I think):

```python
        try:
            path = urllib.parse.urlparse(filepath_or_buffer).path
        except AttributeError:
            path = filepath_or_buffer
```

If it fails on the first try, then it assumes that it's working with a file and not a URL. So we'd have to add an except statement for the specific error that gets thrown with an authentication problem. Alternatively, we could add a check in the first try statement, something like:

```python
        try:
            path = urllib.parse.urlparse(filepath_or_buffer).path
            if 'data8.berkeley.edu' in filepath_or_buffer:
                raise ValueError('data8 filepaths require authentication, host your data on a public server')
        except AttributeError:
            path = filepath_or_buffer
```

Binder support more than just github (although UI for that isn't here yet) - gist, gitlab, raw git, and probably OSF in the future. Internally, binder's URL structure is `<apiVersion>/<provider>/<provider-specific-reference>`, so individual providers can dictate what kinda information they need. For the github provider ('gh') it is '<username>/<repo>/<git-ref>', but for other providers it's different. This should be incorporated into the design somehow from the start IMO, while defaulting to github. 

Perhaps an optional `?provider=` that defaults to github, and combine the repo and ref URL params to a single one that can be passed to the binder api?

While this patch fixes the issue, I'm not a big fan of it, as it effectively changes the behavior of `_record_step_results()`, and if I understand it correctly, with this change, the previously ticked path will go through filter hooks, and be appended to a stash (`new_stashes[goto]`), while it shouldn't filtered at all (since, filtering an already stepped path does not make sense - that path belongs to the past).

The correct solution, as I see it, is to explicitly call `filter(<all_active_paths>)` in `PathGroup.step()`, prior to entering the main loop in that method. You can also call `until(pg)` there, which also makes sure that the `until` method handles the very first step of a path.

Requests:

- please move the pretty-printing stuff onto either the base Analysis class or into angr.misc
- please rename this to something like "Overview" or "Metadata". To us, "static analysis" refers exclusively to analysis that examines program code in a way that is more abstract than emulation. Control-flow recovery, data-flow analysis, and value-set analysis are all examples of static analysis. Depending on who you ask, symbolic execution is a type of static analysis, since it is much more abstract than pure emulation, but in angr we tend to view symbolic execution as a kind of dynamic analysis. This is not examining program code at all, simply aggregating metadata.

You will also want to make sure you're not going to run afoul of the other refactor going on to clean up the knowledge base. This is a pretty prototypical example of something that would be a "view" instead of an "analysis" under the new system, but we don't really have that designation ready yet...

Your PYTHONPATH is a system environment variable that keeps track of the location of all the python packages you have on your system. There is a default location that all python packages are "installed" depending on whether you are on windows/mac/linux. When a python package is "installed" (e.g. via pip), usually all it means is that the code gets put into that default location. Then when you `import package_name`, python looks in the default location for a package with name `package_name`, which it then imports. If python does not find the package in the default location, it will continue looking in any other directories you specify within your PYTHONPATH system environment variable (which, by the way, gets loaded into `sys.path` within python so you can see it there too).

So when you add e.g. `C:/Users/MyUsername/cassiopeia` to your PYTHONPATH and `import cassiopeia`, python will look inside that folder for something to import called `cassiopeia`, which it will find and then successfully import for you to use.

This is not accessible.

The best solution is to use an `<button>` for buttons, as opposed to a `<div>` with listeners. If you can find a way to do that, that would be great: all browsers and assistive tools will do the right thing without any interaction management. An `<a>`nchor is also an acceptable solution, though `<button>` is better. (Note that you can style buttons just like anything else, so they don't have to have that "traditional button look": on this page, for instance, note that "No one—assign yourself", the "status okay" checkmark next to the commit SHA, and "Show all reviewers" are all `<button>`s.)

If for some reason there is no way to get the above to work, then at the very least you'll need to add `tabindex="0"` to the `<div id="main-image-container">` so that it's keyboard-focusable. I'm not sure what Polymer's `on-tap` does; you'll probably need to add an `onkeypress` listener that toggles the actual size when Enter and Space are pressed, as well. You will also need to add `role="button"` to the div. This approach is much less good because it requires emulation of potentially platform-specific functionality (for instance, I use an extension that lets me interact with mouse elements using the keyboard, and I'm not sure that it would work in this case; actual accessibility tools will face similar problems).

@jwhui, @darconeous, and @nibanks 

I'd like to draw your attention to this issue, it is the next one I would like to better address.

This is the next large item I want to push into OpenThread.

Code in openthread currently does this:

`      #include <string.h>
`
To fix missing/problems with string.h - and other related, platforms do some "Gymnastics"
In ./configure land, we "force feed" header files into the compiler.
In windows-land, the openthread-windows-config.h file includes these files.

Doing this header file force feed into the compiler gets ugly in IDE land. Its doable, but ugly.

Another aspect of this is the skill-set of the target developer, some of us propeller heads know well the internal workings of the compiler, and the ide configuration files, etc...  In the broad market, not all developers really understand this, especially noobs, if the goal is to have a mass market for thread - then doing things that are easy and quick to understand, is the better way.

I don't think the gymnastics approach used right now is a good long term solution, and thus I want to put forth what I think is a better choice or method

It comes down to this:

**STEP 1**

OLD CODE: 
```
#include <string.h> /* old code */
```
NEW CODE
```
#include <utils/wrap_string.h> /* use this name instead */
```
The same would apply to  stdbool.h, and stdint.h for windows, and more importantly - the same model can be used for **ALL** future files in the same way, just create "wrap_SOMENAME.h" as needed.

In a perfect world, this new "wrap_string.h" - just includes <string.h>
But we don't live in that world.

An example implementation of 'wrap_string.h' might be like this:

Note: I have this ready to go now, we are using this internally now.
My only questions are, if I push this up, how should this be handled

a)  Separate library? if so what name?
    This would obvious need a bit of tweaking on windows side (a new library to make)

b)   Or integrating into the existing openthread library
    I think this is the correct/better choice, it is more transparent.
   That said, it would require add/remove a few files to the vcxproject files.

c) Or nope, this is not the correct way to solve this.

```
/* copyright statement here */
#if !defined(WRAP_STRING_H)
#define WRAP_STRING_H

#include "wrap_common.h" // common wrapper macros */

/* include the system provided header */
#include <string.h>

/* these are the alternate implementations */
/* See: https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=strlcpy */
WRAP_EXTERN_C size_t missing_strlcpy(char   *dst, const char *src, size_t dstsize);
/* See: https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=strlcat */
WRAP_EXTERN_C size_t missing_strlcat(char   *dst, const char *src, size_t dstsize);
/* See: https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=strnlen */
WRAP_EXTERN_C size_t missing_strnlen(const char *s, size_t maxlen);

/* undefine any compiler supplied function. */
#undef strlcat
#undef strlcpy
#undef strnlen

/* Define these, it just works always by default ... */
#define strlcat missing_strlcat
#define strlcpy missing_strlcpy
#define strnlen missing_strnlen

/* Undefine below - as [er platform */

#if defined(_MSV_VER)
#undef strnlen /* provided by visual studio */
#endif

#if _wrap_ti_arm
/* TI_ARM compiler is missing all */
#endif

#if __linux__
#undef strnlen /* provided by gcc */
/* strlcpy - missing */
/* strlcat  - missing */
#endif

#if _wrap_iar_arm
/* IAR systems for ARM  */
/* does not supply any in all cases */
#endif

#endif  // WRAP_STRING_H

```

+1 from me. Ran into this issue yesterday when writing a tutorial and using an old switch. This PR fixes the issue.

Please merge. :)

Network devices are slow...and you start running into quite a few issues with managing the CLI session (especially when accounting for variation in devices). 

Going fast for executing a single show command and login is probably 2 to 3 seconds (not several hundred milliseconds). Note there are other things that have to happen that are included in that time (turning of paging, verifying you have logged in, got past any banners, etc).

Generally, there are also reliability tradeoffs in going faster i.e. things start to break much more frequently in the CLI interactions.

Also you typically just overlay concurrency on top of this...so yes one device takes a minute to complete, but 500 devices can also take close to a minute to complete (actually slightly more but that is generally due to handling devices that fail).

In general, I think reliability is much more important than performance so I generally am trying to emphasize that.

You can possibly try to go faster by setting `global_delay_factor=.5` (or .2). Even there you might have to get into modifying the source code of Netmiko (as global_delay_factor is mainly intended to go slower and not faster).

the last line "keywords – additional keywords can be found at pyexcel.get_book()" would bring you to the get_book()'s parameters :). 

for 1st part of your code, you can try:

    import pyexcel as p
    p.save_as(file_name="example.xlsx", sheet_name="Sheet2", dest_file_name="example-sheet2.csv")

for 2nd part of your code:

    import pyexcel as p

        sheet.save_as(sheet.name+'.csv')

or:

What's more, alternatively you could choose [pyexcel-cli](https://github.com/pyexcel/pyexcel-cli), which is not well documented so far but you can use help message to get around. For 1st part of your code, you could do this:

    pyexcel transcode --sheet-name Sheet2 example.xlsx example-sheet2.csv

for 2nd part of your code, you could the following instead:

    pyexcel transcode example.xlsx example.csv

For the help with pyexcel-cli, you can do `pyexcel transcode --help`

The full command line options are:

    C:\>pyinstaller m.py --hidden-import pyexcel.plugins.sources.file_input --hidden-import pyexcel.plugins.parsers.excel --hidden-import pyexcel_xls --hidden-import pyexcel_xls.xlsr

with pyexcel v0.5.0 and pyexcel 0.4.0, pyinstaller options become long. The reason is that all plugins were loaded on demand. It means, in your case, only the components mentioned in the hidden imports were imported by Python into memory. When you packaging your application up, pyinstaller could not capture this dynamic behavior.

Lots of refactoring in progress.  Exec statements are not "best practice" anyway, but they work differently in Python 3 vs. Python 2.  

> There is a big difference between exec in Python 2 and exec() in Python 3. You are treating exec as a function, but it really is a statement in Python 2.
> Because of this difference, you cannot change local variables in function scope in Python 3 using exec, even though it was possible in Python 2. Not even previously declared variables.

> By making the name field a static member, aren't we providing a definition without declaration, which has the potential to lead to issues like #770?

It's perfectly fine here because `static constexpr auto signature = ...` is explicitly asking for a compile-time constant. The `constexpr` part ensures that all the individual `type_caster::name` variables will be concatenated at compile-time (no definitions needed). A new variable `signature` will be created for which we do need a definition, and the handy function-scope `static` ensures the compiler will store it in the binary.

What effectively happened with that previous issue is:
```c++
void runtime_foo(py::detail::descr<8, T1, T2> const&); 

runtime_foo(make_caster<T>::name); // ODR-used here => `::name` needs a definition
```
So, mixed run time and compile time. But with `descr`, everything is kept strictly in a `constexpr` context and it's used exclusively by `signature` which will be correctly stored for runtime access.

> This is changing the (public) type_caster interface in the case that someone used their own name(). Do you think it's worth dealing with this (for example, by renaming the static name to type_name and providing a SFINAE function that uses type_name with a name() fallback)? Or should we just consider this an internal detail, since the documented approach doesn't mention name() at all, but just the TYPE_CASTER macro?

Pretty much the last part. Considering it's in the `detail` namespace and only `PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER` is [documented](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced/cast/custom.html), I'd vote for supporting backward compatibility of `PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER` (which this does) but nothing beyond that.

Long term, I think we all agree, it would be good to pull `type_caster` out of the `detail` namespace, stabilize and fully document it. I have another PR pending to get it moving in this direction. As far as I see, this can all be done while maintaining `PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER`, `load()` and `cast()` compatible as is currenly documented, but not the internal details.

> Related, but separate question: do we have an upgrade path or plan for acme dependencies?

This is tricky. The old versions of our dependencies work just fine with older versions of Python and some distros are relying on this behavior. On top of that, conditionally changing our dependencies based on the Python version isn't supported in the old versions of setuptools used in some distros. Because of this, we've been ignoring problems like this in our Python packages and just handling them in `certbot-auto` and distro packages.

See #3455 and https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#id283 for more info. I'm happy to chat about this more if you'd like.

Yes you can use `--expand` without `--force-renewal`. The problem is that you're not listing all domains in the existing certificate. Perhaps we should change the documentation for this flag to say "If an existing cert **is a** subset of the requested names...".

> Also what if an existing certificate covers a superset of the requested names ("covers" to me suggests that would be fine)? It should then expand and add the newly requested domains.

In this case, Certbot would create a new directory in `/etc/letsencrypt/live` for the certificate. I believe this problem is also resolved if we change "covers" to "is a".

Honestly, the `--expand` flag is a bit clunky and supporting use cases like this should be a lot easier in our next release. See #3615 for what we're planning to do.

Interesting. Looking through your log, the only domain that's failing authorization is `login.kristshell.net`. All other domains are succeeding.

What's going on when Certbot tries to authorize your domain using the Apache plugin is it creates a temporary virtual host that responds to a TLS request to your server for the host `<specific hash>.acme.invalid` with a specific self signed certificate. When it tries to do the challenge for `login.kristshell.net`, something goes wrong. In particular, the Let's Encrypt server gets back a certificate containing the domains `cubemail.kristshell.net, erikandre.kristshell.net, login.kristshell.net, ordbok.kristshell.net, sid.kristshell.net, squirrelmail.kristshell.net, webmail.kristshell.net, www.kristshell.net` rather than the self signed certificate. Presumably this is the certificate you're trying to renew.

When Certbot creates this temporary vhost, it tries to find the existing vhost that matches the domain it's trying to solve the challenge for and creates the temporary vhost using the same address and port values in the existing vhost. For `login.kristshell.net` it uses the address and port `*:443` while for all other domains it uses `91.205.184.98:443`. Is this correct? It chooses the vhost based on `ServerName`/`ServerAlias` directives, preferring the most specific match both in terms of the name/alias and server/port. Looking at your existing vhosts, is this `*:443` the correct choice? By creating the vhost this way (whose full contents are in your log), do you know why it conflicted with your existing vhost serving the domains I listed above?

Hopefully this information and questions helps to start to try and track down the problem.

As for having to create a config file for every host/alias that you secure, I'm unaware of another solution to the problem with Apache, however, I'm certainly open to suggestions.

Just some typos and wording changes in the docs.

As discussed in rocketchat it is indeed a good idea to remove the optimization of having a direct transfer for our direct channel neighbors if we are to gain transfer expiration and cancelability for all possible transfer targets.

> a sufficient number of headers where the sum of `available_gas` from these is enough for the other counter party to call unlock on all pending locks, proving that there was enough time to unlock any pending locks.

well I am afraid that just the *sum* wouldn't be enough, instead we would need to show that there would have been enough "slots" for complete unlock transactions (if we need 1000 gas to call unlock once and there were 10 blocks with 100 `available_gas` the transaction would not have been possible).

> The above sort of implies that a "out of gas slot proof" should be added to the unlock function, the unlock transaction would contain a proof that it slipped after the expiration block because of the lack of gas slots, effectively extending the lock's expiration.

Yeah, I think that's right: unlock should allow for a `delay_justification`. But that sounds terrible to prove just in time: Alice tries to unlock lock A (with `expiration=X`) in block `X`; block `X` is full. Now Alice needs to provide the proof and retry in block `X+1`.

> I think it's best to keep this complexity in the choice of the settlement period and lock's expiration instead of shifting it to the smart contract, the gas slot strategy basically allows for a smaller expiration value since the participants can provide a proof to extend the expiration period, instead of using a large expiration that accounts for the worst case (I'm assuming the out of gas slot thing), but I think the larger expiration periods is easier to implement and to reason about

I agree in regards to complexity, but this proposal aims to address the congestion/flood issue [see also https://lightning.network/lightning-network-paper.pdf 3.3.1/Timestop] in a way that does not require wild ass guesses for a "safe" timeout. 
Note, that a safe large timeout would also need to be set ever higher with higher usage of the network, because if more closing channels have to wait until the very end of their `settlement_timeout` for unlocking because of congestion, they will compete for the few last slots, thereby making the problem worse. This proposal aims to avoid such cascading failure, by allowing for a timestop proof (to use the LN lingo here) in the smart contracts.
Noted though, that the lock expiry is independent of the closing block and makes things pretty complex. This will need some research.

> I'm not yet sure what the upside of a (seemingly arbitrary) two-fold type discrimination (type + class) is?

The two-fold type-specifier was initially introduced for easier class inheritance in `marshmallow`, and should represent Baseclass and Childclass. but It shows that it doesn't make things easier by a lot and also creates unneccessary confusion.

I agree on most of your comments. URLs/Endpoints define the type and version and the envelope isn't neccessarily needed. BUT:

> Essentially we would only need the data part of your proposal since the properties of what these data are would be defined by the URI of the endpoint.

This holds true for everything but the Events, and that's the main reason I introduced the envelope with type-specifier.

_Note: The getter-method for events will pop an Event-Queue of recent events empty and return it as a list._

The list contains different types of Events where most have different fields - so the encoding would require support for polymorphism. In order to decode the events on the client-side, it needs to have a type specifier, if we don't have a special endpoint for every type of events.

The initial choice for implementing this was an envelop, which was also included in every other object (like a channel) for consistency.

We could do 2 things:

- poll uniform lists of a specific type from separate endpoints (like `/api/version/events/channel_closed/ GET` )  

- stay with the mixed type list approach but just include the type in the former `data`-dict, and do this only for events:
```python
             {
                "event_type": "channel_settled",
                "netting_channel_address": "0x624c4a42215d63282b5e3f754d7b3d5e542c3842",
                "block_number": 1
             }
```

I'm more in favor of version 2, because version 1 would require some 'server'-side filtering logic and multiple long-polling endpoints.

I'm currently reworking the API-exposure based on the recent experiments with `gevent-websocket`.
The idea is to support 3 communication-procedures, all of which are transported over a single websocket and comply with JSON-RPC-standards:
-  **standard JSON-RPC calls for synchronous methods** that don't rely on callback execution and success-confirmations: the result of the call is directly returned to the websocket.
- **JSON-RPC calls for asynchronous methods** relying on callback execution:
the call itself triggers a method like `transfer_async()` with given arguments.
Internally, a `call_id` is constructed and a callback-function is bound to the `call_id`.
The RPC then returns the `call_id` to the websocket, making it possible to wait for the callback to execute on the client side. The callback will post a success/timeout message (or any other arbitrary information) to the websocket, where the client can process this.

- **push Events** - this probably is the most difficult part, so for now a simplified version of this is planned:
Different getter methods will be implemented in the API,  e.g. `get_balance()` that automatically get triggered when a balance update is deducted in raiden. So for every update the client receives the return value of the getter over the websocket, implying that balance-updates have to be considered. In order to trigger the update, something like `gevent.Event()`'s are needed down the raiden codebase on every relevant state-change.

One part to consider is also that the protocol and messages are non-standard and have to be defined and documented. For easier adaption we should also consider something like a `raiden.js`-client that offers a high-level interface with the raiden-api and handles the event-updates with something like `ReactiveX.js`'s Observables etc.

I actually favor the second option, python's packaging is by itself so I would like to remove any magic from `setup.py`. Yes we will have some duplication, but it will be just a few lines of dependencies. Thoughts?

```
# Binary compatibility version number. This number is increased whenever the
# C-API is changed such that binary compatibility is broken, i.e. whenever a
# recompile of extension modules is needed.
C_ABI_VERSION = 0x01000009

# Minor API version.  This number is increased whenever a change is made to the
# C-API -- whether it breaks binary compatibility or not.  Some changes, such
# as adding a function pointer to the end of the function table, can be made
# without breaking binary compatibility.  In this case, only the C_API_VERSION
# (*not* C_ABI_VERSION) would be increased.  Whenever binary compatibility is
# broken, both C_API_VERSION and C_ABI_VERSION should be increased.
#
# 0x00000008 - 1.7.x
# 0x00000009 - 1.8.x
# 0x00000009 - 1.9.x
# 0x0000000a - 1.10.x
# 0x0000000a - 1.11.x
# 0x0000000a - 1.12.x
# 0x0000000b - 1.13.x
# 0x0000000c - 1.14.x
C_API_VERSION = 0x0000000c

OK, here is what has been implemented so far: 

1. A `%taskinfo` magic has been implemented.
2. Task ID in task list is now clickable.
3. Click the ID will send a `task-info` message to the kernel, which in turn triggers a `sos status task_id -v3` command from the task engine. The command could be executed on the remote host.
4. The result will be sent to the frontend through message `task-info`.
5. The frontend displays the message in the side panel.

So we are not doing anything fancy here. No drop down stuff, and the output could be formatted nicer, but we largely have what we want.

Hi,

I managed to get an updated Debian package working in Debian without additional dependencies to package (but that's my first real Debian package).
I used a separate Debian repository with its very own history as it seemed easier to handle (for example, the released source is not just a git archive of the tag).

The source package is at https://github.com/amurzeau/streamlink-debian and based on @breunigs work in #80. Changes I have made are available here: https://github.com/amurzeau/streamlink-debian/compare/95712f7d71417079fde126fc210352f9c259f475...master

Some indications :
* I choose to only make python3 packages as some of the python-backport dependencies are not available at all in Debian (like `backports.shutil_which`)
* I removed copyright information about non existing file `src/streamlink/packages/pbs.py` and added one for `src/streamlink/packages/requests_file.py` and some specific files in the sphinx rtd theme
* I updated quilt patches to the 0.5.0 streamlink version and changed a bit the patches headers so gbp pq keep them
* I changed the `debian/rules` file to enable usage of `pycountry` and `pycrypto` as these libs are the only alternative available currently in debian (`iso3166` and `pycryptodome` are not available)
* I added a patch to `src/streamlink/utils/l10n.py` so older pycountry that does not have the `lookup` method works too (the python3-pycountry package in Debian doesn't have this method)
* I added a `.travis.yml` file so travis build and test packages using sbuild
* I disabled compression on font files so the font-awesome icons works when browsing the locally installed doc in the `python3-streamlink-doc` package

@breunigs or others, let me know if I got something wrong in my changes but everything should be Ok :)

Additionally, I've put binary and source packages in a github repo using gh-pages so it can be used like a normal apt repository: https://github.com/amurzeau/streamlink-apt-repo.
These binary package are generated with sbuild. deb files are the same exact file as the one built by travis as the build is reproducible.

I've found bintray to be a possible alternative, but it requires to give it a private GPG key to have a secure APT repo (bintray itself manages the automatic signature of Release file and maybe other).

Here's a tentative outline for the split of `jupyter-js-widgets`:

* `@jupyter-widgets/base` - Base widgets, such as Widget, DOMWidget, LabeledDOMWidget, LayoutWidget. These are meant to be subclassed. This would also include the widget serializer, which is of general use.
  - `nativeview.ts`
  - `widget.ts`
  - `widget_layout.ts`
  - `utils.ts`
  - nominally, the output widget (i.e., the module for the output widget will be this namespace, and widget managers are responsible for providing it and special-casing it.)
* `@jupyter-widgets/manager` - the base widget manager that can be subclassed, depends on `@jupyter-widgets/base`
  - `manager-base.ts`
  - `services-shim.ts`
* `@jupyter-widgets/core` - Core jupyter widgets, such as sliders, boxes, etc. Basically, anything that now inherits from the core widget. The version number of this package tracks compatibility with the wire format, *not* the class implementations. Depends on `@jupyter-widgets/base`.
  - `phosphor/` directory of custom phosphor widgets
  - `widget_bool.ts`
  - `widget_box.ts`
  - `widget_button.ts`
  - `widget_color.ts`
  - `widget_controller.ts`
  - `widget_core.ts`
  - `widget_date.ts`
  - `widget_float.ts`
  - `widget_image.ts`
  - `widget_int.ts`
  - `widget_link.ts`
  - `widget_selection.ts`
  - `widget_selectioncontainer.ts`
  - `widget_string.ts`
  - `widget_style.ts`
* `@jupyter-widgets/embed-manager` - the embedded widget manager, depends on `@jupyter-widgets/manager`
  - `src-embed` directory
* `@jupyter-widgets/schema` - renamed from `jupyter-widgets-schema`
* `@jupyter-widgets/jupyterlab` - renamed `@jupyterlab/nbwidgets`


We keep the `jupyter-js-widgets` package, which imports from the other packages to provide compatibility. This package is deprecated.

The widgetsnbextension node package is a private package, not published.

from ipywidgets import *

from ipywidgets import *

Ah, I see! You're talking about wall clock / absolute time, like "January 7, 2018, 17:48:12.5839". All of Trio's time-keeping functionality is (currently) about monotonic / relative time. These are very similar and easily confused, but actually pretty different when you look at them carefully.

Wall clock time is matched to the calendar, so it makes sense to say things like "what's today's date", "what's the current day of the week", etc.; you have to think about complications like DST and leap seconds; and it's possible for a computer to be confused about the current wall clock time so you have to be prepared for the time to be set, sometimes to a time in the past. In Python you normally get this from `time.time` or `datetime.now`.

Monotonic time is simpler in most ways: it doesn't care about absolute time ("January 7 at 17:39:32"), it only cares about relative time ("this event was 1837.32491 seconds after that one"). So it doesn't try to keep track of its relationship to any absolute epoch – the actual values are arbitrary, the only guarantee is that if you check the time twice and then subtract those values, that difference tells you how many seconds have passed. Since it doesn't have to care about keeping in sync with the earth's rotation, it doesn't have to support stepping backwards, and it can guarantee that it increases by N every N seconds. (POSIX time can't make this guarantee even if we ignore clock setting, because it goes backwards during leap seconds!) Hence the name "monotonic" time. OTOH, in standard operating system implementations of monotonic time, the clock does stop while the computer is suspended, which is totally different from absolute time. In Python you normally get this from `time.monotonic`.

There are even other more complicated options too – according to `man clock_gettime`, Linux currently has 6 different kinds of clocks you can query... but plain old monotonic time like I described above is the one that everyone uses for things like I/O timeouts, and in particular is the kind of time that `epoll` and friends use. So that's what's baked into Trio, and used by `trio.sleep` and timeouts, and that's what `MockClock` is mocking.

Since `MockClock` is intended to be predictable for testing, and since the epoch for monotonic time is totally arbitrary, it always starts at time 0.0. (And Trio's real clock actually uses a randomized epoch, to help catch the common bug where someone uses `time.monotonic` instead of `trio.current_time`.)

There is an issue option for possibly adding wall clock / absolute time support to Trio: #173. (It might be better as a library than as part of the Trio core.) I haven't thought too much about the details of how that API might look, but definitely you'd want to have some kind of testing capability, and definitely you'd want to be able to set it to arbitrary times, advance by arbitrary amounts, etc.

Do you think it's really the ability to control the speed of the clock that you want, or mainly just absolute clock support? For example, if I had set up a job to run "every Saturday at midnight", I might write a test like:

1. Start running at virtual "Saturday 6 pm"
2. Move the clock forward to "Saturday 11:59pm"
3. Assert that my job hasn't run yet
4. Move the clock forward to "Sunday 12:01am"
5. Assert that my job just ran

But that doesn't involve ever running the clock at any multiple of real time, just manually stepping it. Do you have an example in mind where running it normally is useful?

I made a bunch of edits.  Ready to pass back to you.  Or to anyone else who wants to take a swipe at it.

Unless somebody volunteers to do a custom spline implementation, I think adding GSL with the understanding we shouldn't use it unless necessary is probably the right call for now.  We really shouldn't have anything related to NR any longer than we have to.

A custom implementation also has the potential to be a lot more user-friendly in C++; we could wrap the GSL C interface in a nice C++ layer too, of course, but for something as simple as splines that may be similar to the amount of work it would take to do a custom C++ implementation.

So even if we do need GSL for now, I'd also eventually like to see us phase it out if we don't run into something harder than splines that we want from it.  But getting the NR out should be a higher priority.
